genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces acid chloride all the way to primary alcohol; DIBALH or H2/Pd-BaSO4 (Rosenmund) stops at aldehyde — reductant choice is a genuine lever
NaBH4 in EtOH reduces acid chloride to alcohol; NaBH4 in other contexts (e.g., with aldehydes/ketones) is milder and does not reduce acid chlorides — solvent/conditions matter
common_traps: Assuming LiAlH4 stops at aldehyde for acid chlorides (it does not; aldehyde intermediate is immediately reduced further)
Confusing this with ester reduction by DIBALH (which stops at aldehyde) or LiAlH4 (which goes to alcohol)
acid_chloride_to_alcohol_1lialh4lithi.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reductant selectivity: LiAlH4 vs DIBALH vs NaBH4 vs Rosenmund conditions)

genuine_levers: NaN3/H3O+ sequence is a specific Curtius rearrangement pathway; using NaN3/ROH directly gives urethane (carbamate) instead of amine
The rearrangement step (acyl azide to isocyanate) is concerted and stereospecific — any stereochemistry at the α-carbon is retained (migration with retention)
common_traps: Confusing Curtius (NaN3/H3O+) with Schmidt (HN3/H2SO4) — both give amines but mechanisms and intermediates differ
Forgetting the loss of CO2 in the final hydrolysis step, leading to a product with an extra carbon (carboxylic acid) instead of amine
Mistaking the isocyanate intermediate for the final product
acid_chloride_to_amine_nan3h3o.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism-driven product prediction: acyl azide → isocyanate → amine vs. trapping with alcohol)

genuine_levers: AlCl₃ generates acylium ion (R''CO⁺) which attacks the ether oxygen — Friedel-Crafts-like acylation of an ether, not an arene
Anhydrous conditions required — AlCl₃ is moisture-sensitive; water would hydrolyze the acid chloride
common_traps: Confusing this with standard Friedel-Crafts acylation of an aromatic ring — the electrophile attacks the ether O, not an aromatic C
Assuming the ester forms via nucleophilic acyl substitution (e.g., alcohol + acid chloride) — the mechanism here is electrophilic attack on the ether
acid_chloride_to_ester_alclfriedelc.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: electrophilic acylium attack on ether oxygen vs. aromatic acylation vs. nucleophilic acyl substitution)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Gilman reagent (R₂CuLi) stops at ketone; Grignard reagent (RMgX) over-adds to give tertiary alcohol
acid_chloride_to_ketone_dimethylcupr.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the ketone product becomes chiral (i.e., R' and R are different and the carbonyl carbon becomes a stereocenter upon further reaction, but the ketone itself is achiral)
common_traps: Mistaking Gilman reagent for Grignard reagent leads to wrong product (tertiary alcohol)
Assuming all organometallics (RMgX, RLi, R₂CuLi) behave identically with acid chlorides
acid_chloride_to_ketone_dimethylcupr.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (organometallic reagent selectivity: Gilman vs Grignard with acid chlorides)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: conc. H₂SO₄/heat (E1, Saytzeff) vs POCl₃/pyridine (E2, Saytzeff) vs bulky base like t-BuOK (E2, Hofmann) vs ThO₂/Δ (E1cb, Hofmann)
Substrate structure: acyclic (Saytzeff applies) vs rigid cyclic (anti-periplanar geometry overrides Saytzeff)
Leaving group ability: poor LG (NR₃⁺, F⁻) favors Hofmann product via carbanion-like TS
common_traps: Assuming all dehydrating agents give Saytzeff product (ThO₂ gives Hofmann)
Applying Saytzeff rule to rigid cyclic substrates (anti-periplanar geometry determines product)
Confusing t-BuO⁻ (E2, Hofmann) with t-BuOH (E1, Saytzeff)
Thinking E1 gives a mixture of regioisomers (it gives only Saytzeff)
Assuming ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff product (it gives Hofmann via Ei mechanism)
alcohol_to_alkene_conchso.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and substrate control of regiochemistry: Saytzeff vs Hofmann vs anti-periplanar)

genuine_levers: High temperature (450°C) and Al2O3 catalyst drive dehydration and subsequent amine synthesis; lower temperatures or different catalysts (e.g., H2SO4) would give ether or alkene only
Ammonia source (NH3) is implied; using a primary or secondary amine instead would yield a substituted amine (e.g., R2NH gives tertiary amine directly)
alcohol_to_amine_al2o3450c.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting alcohol is chiral and the reaction proceeds via SN1-type substitution with racemization; high-temperature conditions typically lead to loss of chirality
common_traps: Assuming this is a simple nucleophilic substitution (SN1/SN2) — it is a high-temperature catalytic process involving dehydration first
Overlooking carbocation rearrangements (1,2-shifts) that can change the carbon skeleton before amine formation
Mistaking the product for a primary amine — conditions favor thermodynamic tertiary amine with excess alcohol
alcohol_to_amine_al2o3450c.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism shift: dehydration-amination vs. direct substitution; rearrangement possibility)

genuine_levers: CH₂N₂ methylates O-H (phenol > alcohol) — reagent choice is a genuine lever; using CH₃I/NaOH gives same product but different mechanism/conditions
CH₂N₂ selectivity: RCOOH >> ArOH > ROH (pKa order) — reactivity depends on acidity of OH/COOH
common_traps: CH₂N₂ is NOT a methylating agent for alkyl halides or aromatic rings — it specifically methylates acidic O-H (phenols, carboxylic acids)
CH₂N₂ is explosive and toxic — students often forget the hazard and treat it as a benign reagent like CH₃I
alcohol_to_carbene_chndiazometh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: CH₂N₂ vs other methylating agents; mechanism via proton abstraction/SN2)

genuine_levers: Strong oxidant (KMnO4/H+, Jones) oxidizes primary alcohol all the way to carboxylic acid; mild oxidant (PCC) stops at aldehyde
Secondary alcohols are NOT oxidized to carboxylic acids under these conditions — they stop at ketone
common_traps: Confusing KMnO4 with PCC — PCC stops at aldehyde, KMnO4 over-oxidizes to acid
Thinking secondary alcohols can be oxidized to carboxylic acids by strong oxidants (they cannot under standard conditions)

genuine_levers: RCOCl + alcohol → ester (fast, irreversible); RCOOH + alcohol (Fischer) → ester (slow, equilibrium, acid-catalyzed) — reagent choice is a genuine lever
Base (pyridine/Et₃N) is essential to neutralize HCl; its absence leads to poor yield or side reactions
Acyl chloride route is suitable for acid-sensitive or sterically hindered alcohols; Fischer esterification fails here
alcohol_to_ester_rcocl.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alcohol carbon is chiral; the reaction proceeds with retention of configuration at the alcohol carbon (SN2-like at carbonyl, but double inversion via tetrahedral intermediate gives overall retention)
common_traps: Confusing acyl chloride route (irreversible) with Fischer esterification (reversible, acid-catalyzed) — they require different conditions and give different yields
Omitting the base (pyridine) leads to HCl accumulation, which can protonate the alcohol or cause rearrangements
Assuming acyl anhydride is as reactive as acyl chloride — it is slower and may require different conditions
alcohol_to_ester_rcocl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: acyl chloride vs carboxylic acid vs anhydride; mechanism discrimination)

genuine_levers: Na/RX (Williamson) = SN2 on primary R'X; conc. H2SO4/heat = intermolecular dehydration (SN2) for symmetrical ethers; acid + tertiary alcohol gives alkene (E1), NOT ether
alcohol_to_ether_na.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the SN2 step occurs at a stereocenter (inversion of configuration); if the alkyl halide (R'X) is chiral, the ether product will have a new stereocenter
common_traps: Using tertiary alkyl halide in Williamson synthesis leads to elimination (E2), not substitution
Applying acid-catalyzed conditions to a tertiary alcohol yields alkene, not ether
Confusing Na/RX (ether synthesis) with NaBH4 (reduction)
alcohol_to_ether_na.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (condition discrimination: Williamson SN2 vs acid-catalyzed dehydration vs elimination)

genuine_levers: Oxidant choice for secondary alcohol: PCC, Jones (CrO₃/H₂SO₄), Swern, or Oppenauer all give ketone; primary alcohol with PCC gives aldehyde, not ketone — substrate identity is a genuine lever
common_traps: PCC does NOT oxidise tertiary alcohols — if starting material is tertiary, this step gives no reaction
PCC does NOT oxidise primary alcohols to ketones (stops at aldehyde) — misidentifying primary alcohol as secondary is a trap
alcohol_to_ketone_pcc.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant selectivity: PCC vs KMnO4 vs Jones for primary vs secondary alcohols)

genuine_levers: Choice of sulfonyl chloride: TsCl gives tosylate (OTs), MsCl gives mesylate (OMs), Tf2O gives triflate (OTf) — different leaving group abilities
Pyridine acts as base to scavenge HCl; other bases (e.g., Et3N) can be used but pyridine is standard
Reaction with a chiral alcohol proceeds with retention of configuration at the carbon — stereochemistry is preserved
alcohol_to_substituted_benzene_4methylbe.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting alcohol is chiral; the reaction proceeds with retention of configuration, so it does not create new stereocenters but preserves existing ones
common_traps: Confusing this with an EAS reaction — the note about 'EAS' and 'regiochemistry' is a trap; this is a nucleophilic substitution on sulfur, not electrophilic aromatic substitution on the benzene ring
Thinking the reaction inverts stereochemistry — it proceeds with retention via a two-step mechanism (formation of a sulfonate ester)
Using TsCl without a base leads to poor yield due to HCl buildup
alcohol_to_substituted_benzene_4methylbe.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the reaction as a functional group transformation to create a good leaving group, not an EAS); Structural (if the alcohol is chiral, stereochemistry is a key point)

genuine_levers: Temperature is a genuine lever: <110°C gives sulfonate; 110–170°C gives ether; >170°C gives alkene (dehydration)
Reagent identity: conc. H₂SO₄ directly esterifies OH; other sulfonyl chlorides (TsCl, MsCl) with base give different sulfonate esters (ROTs, ROMs)
common_traps: Confusing this low-T sulfonate formation with high-T dehydration to alkene
Assuming the product is a symmetrical sulfate ester (R-O-SO₂-O-R) instead of the alkyl hydrogen sulfate (R-OSO₃H)
Forgetting the reaction is reversible on warming
alcohol_to_sulfonate_conchso.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature control of product: sulfonate vs ether vs alkene)

genuine_levers: Choice of diol (ROH) determines acetal structure; ethylene glycol gives cyclic acetal, methanol gives dimethyl acetal
Anhydrous conditions are essential for formation; water presence drives equilibrium toward hydrolysis back to aldehyde
common_traps: Students forget the acid catalyst (dry HCl/p-TsOH) is required for both formation AND hydrolysis
Mistaking acetal for stable under acidic conditions — it is stable to base but hydrolyzes in aqueous acid
Assuming the reaction is irreversible; it is an equilibrium driven by removal of water
aldehyde_to_acetal_roh2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (protecting group logic: acetal stable to base, labile to acid; mechanism reversibility)

genuine_levers: NaBH₄ vs LiAlH₄ selectivity: NaBH₄ reduces aldehydes/ketones only; LiAlH₄ reduces all C=O (esters, acids, amides) — reagent choice is a genuine lever
aldehyde_to_alcohol_nabh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the aldehyde is prochiral (RCHO where R ≠ H) and the reduction creates a chiral alcohol; achiral aldehydes (e.g., CH₃CHO) give achiral alcohols
common_traps: NaBH₄ does NOT reduce esters, carboxylic acids, or amides — a common JEE trap is to assume it reduces all carbonyls like LiAlH₄
NaBH₄ is used in protic solvent (EtOH/H₂O); LiAlH₄ requires anhydrous conditions (dry ether) — confusing the conditions leads to wrong product or no reaction
aldehyde_to_alcohol_nabh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: NaBH₄ vs LiAlH₄); Structural only if the product is genuinely chiral

genuine_levers: Dilute NaOH + heat → aldol condensation (dehydrated α,β-unsaturated aldehyde); Cold dilute NaOH → aldol addition (β-hydroxy aldehyde) — temperature is a genuine lever
Self-condensation of acetaldehyde vs. cross-aldol with a different carbonyl partner — substrate choice is a genuine lever
common_traps: Forgetting that heat is required for the dehydration step to get the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde
Writing the product as the β-hydroxy aldehyde when the conditions specify heat
Mistaking this for Cannizzaro reaction (acetaldehyde has α-H, so it undergoes aldol, not Cannizzaro)
aldehyde_to_aldehyde_naohdilute51.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature control: addition vs. condensation); Structural (recognizing self-aldol product)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Clemmensen (acidic) vs Wolff-Kishner (basic) — both give alkane but choice depends on substrate acid/base sensitivity
Clemmensen reduces only C=O to CH2; Wolff-Kishner reduces both C=O and C=C (exception case)
common_traps: Assuming Clemmensen proceeds via alcohol intermediate (it does not; it's a direct reduction via carbenoid)
Confusing Wolff-Kishner as only reducing C=O (it reduces C=C as well, which is an exception)
aldehyde_to_alkane_znhg.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent choice based on substrate sensitivity; exception case for Wolff-Kishner)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: NaBH3CN reduces imine/iminium selectively at pH 6-7, leaving other carbonyls intact; NaBH4 would reduce the original aldehyde.
Ammonia source: NH3 yields a primary amine; using a primary amine (RNH2) yields a secondary amine.
pH control: Imine formation requires mildly acidic conditions (pH 4-5); NaBH3CN reduction works at pH 6-7 to avoid decomposition.
aldehyde_to_amine_h2ptornabh3c.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting aldehyde is chiral (alpha-chiral aldehyde) or if the imine intermediate is prochiral; reduction of the imine creates a new stereocenter at the former carbonyl carbon.
common_traps: Using NaBH4 instead of NaBH3CN for reduction — NaBH4 would reduce the original aldehyde to an alcohol, not the imine to an amine.
Assuming the reaction gives a tertiary amine — reductive amination with NH3 stops at primary amine; a secondary amine source is needed for a secondary amine product.
Forgetting the two-step sequence (imine formation then reduction) and drawing a direct addition product.
aldehyde_to_amine_h2ptornabh3c.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: NaBH3CN vs NaBH4; mechanism/step sequence); Structural (if a chiral amine product is formed)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Tollens'/Fehling's/Benedict's oxidise ONLY aldehydes (distinguishing test from ketones); KMnO4/K2Cr2O7 oxidise both aldehydes and oxidisable alcohols
Substrate: Ketones are NOT oxidised by Tollens'/Fehling's (except α-hydroxy ketones) — a key diagnostic lever
Formic acid (HCOOH) gives positive Tollens' test (oxidised to CO2) — a specific substrate exception
common_traps: Confusing Tollens' (Ag mirror) with Fehling's (Cu2O red ppt) — both are for aldehydes but different metals
Forgetting that aromatic aldehydes (no α-H) undergo Cannizzaro with conc. alkali, NOT oxidation to acid
Thinking ketones give a positive Tollens' or Fehling's test (they do not, except α-hydroxy ketones)
aldehyde_to_carboxylic_acid_kmnoortollen.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity for distinguishing aldehydes/ketones/alcohols; exception of Cannizzaro for no α-H aldehydes)

genuine_levers: Aldehydes react faster than ketones (sterics/electronics) — substrate choice is a genuine lever
Equilibrium is reversible — use of excess CN⁻ drives reaction forward; conditions matter
Chiral product (racemic) unless asymmetric catalyst used — stereochemistry is a lever
aldehyde_to_cyanohydrin_hcn.creates_stereocenters: true — a new stereocenter is created at the carbonyl carbon upon nucleophilic addition, giving a racemic mixture
common_traps: Using HCN alone (no CN⁻) is slow/ineffective — must generate CN⁻ in situ (e.g., NaCN + acid)
Assuming reaction is irreversible — equilibrium can limit yield
Forgetting that ketones also react (just slower) — not exclusive to aldehydes
aldehyde_to_cyanohydrin_hcn.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate reactivity: aldehyde vs ketone; mechanism: nucleophilic addition under correct pH)

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces aldehydes, ketones, esters, acids, nitriles; NaBH4 reduces only aldehydes and ketones — reagent choice is a genuine lever
aldehyde_to_diol_lahallylalco.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the aldehyde carbon is prochiral and the reduction creates a chiral alcohol; check if R group is different from H
common_traps: LiAlH4 requires anhydrous conditions (dry ether) and a proton source (H2O workup) — writing it in water is a common mistake
Confusing LiAlH4 (strong reducing agent) with KMnO4 (oxidizing agent) is a classic trap
aldehyde_to_diol_lahallylalco.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: LiAlH4 vs NaBH4 vs oxidizing agents)

genuine_levers: PCl5 converts C=O to CCl2 (geminal dichloride); other halogenating agents (e.g., SOCl2) give acid chlorides from carboxylic acids, not gem-dihalides from aldehydes/ketones
common_traps: Confusing PCl5 with PCl3 (PCl3 gives alkyl halides from alcohols, not gem-dihalides)
Assuming the product is a vicinal dihalide (CCl-CCl) instead of a geminal dihalide (CCl2)
Forgetting that the reverse reaction (gem-dihalide + base) can yield an alkyne or ketone, not the original aldehyde
aldehyde_to_geminal_dihalide_pcl5.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: PCl5 vs other halogenating agents for carbonyl conversion)

genuine_levers: Primary amine (RNH2) gives imine; secondary amine (R2NH) gives enamine — amine class is a genuine lever
Aldehydes react faster than ketones due to sterics — substrate steric bulk is a genuine lever
pH 4–5 is optimal; too acidic protonates amine (kills nucleophilicity), too basic slows dehydration — pH control is a genuine lever
common_traps: Using a secondary amine (R2NH) and expecting an imine — the correct product is an enamine
Drawing a carbocation intermediate — the mechanism proceeds via tetrahedral carbinolamine, not a carbocation
Forgetting the acid catalysis role — acid catalyses the dehydration step, but too much acid stops the reaction
aldehyde_to_imine_rnh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (amine class selectivity: 1° vs 2° amine; pH control of nucleophilicity vs dehydration)

genuine_levers: Aldehyde must have NO alpha-hydrogen (e.g., HCHO, PhCHO, (CH3)3CCHO) — presence of alpha-H leads to aldol condensation instead
Cross-Cannizzaro with HCHO: HCHO is always oxidised to formate, forcing the other aldehyde to be reduced to alcohol
common_traps: Applying Cannizzaro to aldehydes with alpha-H (e.g., CH3CHO) — these undergo aldol condensation with base
Forgetting the stoichiometry: 2 moles of aldehyde produce 1 mole each of carboxylate and alcohol
Mistaking the product as a single carboxylic acid — it is a disproportionation reaction yielding two different products
aldehyde_to_substituted_benzene_naohaque.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: no alpha-H requirement; product identification in disproportionation)

genuine_levers: HIO4 cleaves ONLY vicinal (1,2) diols; 1,3-diols are NOT cleaved — diol spacing is a genuine lever
Pb(OAc)4 is an alternative reagent for the same transformation, used in non-aqueous conditions — reagent choice is a lever
Each diol carbon becomes a carbonyl: if it had two H's → HCHO (formaldehyde); one H and one alkyl → aldehyde; two alkyls → ketone — substituent pattern determines product identity
common_traps: Applying HIO4 to a non-vicinal diol (e.g., 1,3-diol) expecting cleavage — it does NOT react
Forgetting that the C-C bond is broken, resulting in TWO separate carbonyl products, not one molecule
Mistaking the product: a terminal diol (e.g., CH2OH-CH2OH) yields two HCHO molecules, not a dialdehyde
diol_to_aldehyde_hio4periodic.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (specificity for vicinal diols; product prediction from substituent pattern)

genuine_levers: Molecular formula is fixed (C5H12); the lever is the carbon skeleton connectivity (straight chain vs branched)
Boiling point comparison: more branching → lower surface area → weaker London forces → lower boiling point
Thermodynamic stability comparison: more branched isomer (neopentane) is more stable (lower heat of combustion)
common_traps: Mistaking this for a chemical reaction — it is a conceptual comparison of isomers; no bonds are broken or formed at room temperature
Incorrectly stating the number of chain isomers for C5H12 (there are 3, not 2 or 4)
Confusing boiling point trend: more branching leads to lower boiling point, not higher
alkane_to_alkane_samemolecula.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (isomer identification and property comparison based on branching)

genuine_levers: Halogen choice (Cl₂ vs Br₂) determines selectivity: Cl₂ is less selective (tertiary > secondary > primary), Br₂ is highly selective (tertiary >> secondary >> primary)
Radical mechanism (X₂, hν) vs ionic mechanism (e.g., NBS, hν for allylic bromination) — different products
alkane_to_alkyl_halide_x.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the halogenated carbon becomes chiral (i.e., if the radical intermediate is chiral or if substitution creates a chiral center); achiral starting alkanes yield racemic mixtures if a stereocenter is formed
common_traps: Assuming ionic mechanism (e.g., SN2) — this is a radical chain reaction
Forgetting that all C-H bonds compete — product mixture depends on C-H bond type and halogen selectivity
Mistaking Br₂/hν for Br₂ in dark (ionic addition to alkenes) — conditions are critical
alkane_to_alkyl_halide_x.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: radical vs ionic; halogen selectivity: Cl₂ vs Br₂)

genuine_levers: Catalyst choice: Al2O3-Cr2O3 for aromatization vs Pt/Al2O3 (platforming) for industrial scale; both give benzene
Temperature: ~600°C required for aromatization; lower temperatures may give cyclohexane or incomplete dehydrogenation
Substrate chain length: n-hexane (C6) cyclizes to benzene; shorter alkanes (C5) cannot form benzene; longer alkanes (C7+) may give toluene or xylenes
common_traps: Confusing this with catalytic cracking (alkane → smaller alkanes/alkenes) — this is aromatization, not bond cleavage
Assuming a simple one-step mechanism — it is a multi-step process (dehydrogenation → cyclization → further dehydrogenation)
Forgetting the H2 atmosphere or removal of H2 as a driving force for the equilibrium
alkane_to_benzene_al2o3cr2o3.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (catalyst and temperature specificity for aromatization vs other reforming processes)

genuine_levers: Length of the dicarboxylate chain (n in NaOOC-(CH2)n-COONa) determines ring size: n=3 → cyclopropane, n=4 → cyclobutane, n=5 → cyclopentane
common_traps: Confusing intramolecular Kolbe (alkane → cycloalkane) with intermolecular Kolbe (two carboxylates → alkane)
Forgetting that the product is a cycloalkane, not a straight-chain alkane
Incorrectly applying the standard Kolbe rule (2RCOO⁻ → R-R) to this intramolecular case without considering ring closure
alkane_to_cycloalkane_electrolysis.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mapping dicarboxylate chain length to specific cycloalkane product)

genuine_levers: Catalyst choice (Pd/C, Pt, Ni, Raney-Ni) affects rate but NOT product — all give alkane via syn addition
Degree of substitution (mono- > di- > tri- > tetra-substituted) affects rate — more hindered alkene reacts slower
alkene_to_alkane_h.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alkene carbons become chiral centers in the alkane (i.e., if each carbon bears three different groups after addition); otherwise false
common_traps: Assuming anti addition (like Br2) — H2/Pd is SYN addition
Writing a carbocation intermediate (no rearrangement occurs; mechanism is surface adsorption/addition)
alkene_to_alkane_h.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (syn addition stereochemistry, especially for cyclic alkenes); Structural (rate order based on substitution)

genuine_levers: Regiochemistry is Markovnikov (H adds to less-substituted C, X adds to more-substituted C) — anti-Markovnikov addition (e.g., with peroxides) gives a different product
Reactivity order HX: HI > HBr > HCl — this can be a lever for selectivity in competitive reactions
Carbocation intermediate can rearrange if a more stable carbocation is possible via hydride/alkyl shift — this changes the product connectivity
alkene_to_alkyl_halide_hx.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter is created at the carbon where X adds, but the product is racemic due to non-stereoselective attack by X⁻ on the planar carbocation; chirality only exists if the carbon bearing X becomes a chiral center (i.e., has four different substituents)
common_traps: Writing anti-Markovnikov addition as the default product (common mistake with HBr/peroxide memory interference)
Assuming stereospecific addition (the product is racemic, not a single enantiomer or diastereomer)
Forgetting carbocation rearrangement possibilities, especially with 3° carbocation adjacent to a 2° center
alkene_to_alkyl_halide_hx.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry: Markovnikov vs anti-Markovnikov); Structural (if rearrangement occurs)

genuine_levers: Hot, acidic, concentrated KMnO4 cleaves C=C to give carboxylic acids; cold, dilute KMnO4 gives syn-diol (no cleavage) — temperature/concentration is a genuine lever
Internal alkene (R-CH=CH-R') → two carboxylic acids (R-COOH + R'-COOH); terminal alkene (R-CH=CH2) → R-COOH + CO2 (HCOOH oxidised further) — substitution pattern determines product count and identity
Ozonolysis (O3 then oxidative workup) gives identical products; reductive ozonolysis (O3/Zn) gives aldehydes/ketones — oxidant/workup choice is a genuine lever
common_traps: Applying cold dilute KMnO4 conditions (syn-dihydroxylation) to a hot concentrated oxidative cleavage problem
Forgetting that terminal alkene gives CO2 (via formic acid) with excess oxidant, not a stable carboxylic acid
Confusing this with KMnO4 oxidation of alcohols (primary alcohol → carboxylic acid, not aldehyde)
alkene_to_carboxylic_acid_hotkmno4acid.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent condition discrimination: hot conc. vs cold dil. KMnO4; product prediction from alkene substitution)

genuine_levers: Markovnikov regiochemistry — the OR group adds to the more substituted carbon of the alkene
The nucleophile is the alcohol (ROH) used in the reaction, determining the OR group in the product ether
alkene_to_ether_hgoacrohorhg.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carbon receiving the OR group becomes a stereocenter (i.e., was originally a terminal alkene carbon that becomes chiral); typically not a focus
common_traps: Mistaking the overall reaction as anti-Markovnikov — it is Markovnikov addition of OR
Confusing this with hydroboration-oxidation (which gives anti-Markovnikov alcohol)
Forgetting the demercuration (NaBH4) step and leaving the HgOAc group in the product
alkene_to_ether_hgoacrohorhg.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry: Markovnikov addition of OR via oxymercuration)

genuine_levers: Regiochemistry: H2O attacks the more substituted carbon of the bromonium ion (Markovnikov-type for OH)
Stereochemistry: anti addition of Br and OH across the double bond
Solvent effect: In aqueous Br2, H2O is the nucleophile; in Br2/NaCl, Cl- can attack giving a bromo-chloro product
alkene_to_halohydrin_br.creates_stereocenters: conditional — creates two new stereocenters (C-Br and C-OH) via anti addition, resulting in a racemic mixture or meso compound depending on alkene substitution
common_traps: Forgetting anti addition and drawing syn addition
Incorrect regiochemistry: placing OH on the less substituted carbon
Mistaking the nucleophile: writing Br- attack instead of H2O attack in aqueous conditions
alkene_to_halohydrin_br.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry and stereochemistry from bromonium ion mechanism)

genuine_levers: Anti addition — stereochemistry of product determined by anti attack on bridged halonium ion
E/Z geometry of starting alkene determines product stereochemistry (meso vs racemic mixture)
common_traps: Assuming syn addition (like OsO4) — this is anti addition
Forgetting bridged halonium ion intermediate and drawing a simple carbocation
Mixing up 'threo' and 'erythro' diastereomer outcomes from cis/trans alkenes
alkene_to_vicinal_dihalide_x.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive — classic test of anti addition stereochemistry and E/Z propagation

genuine_levers: SN2 mechanism for primary/secondary alkyl halides → alcohol with inversion of configuration
Tertiary alkyl halides under same conditions (aq. NaOH) undergo E2 elimination → alkene, NOT alcohol
Solvent choice: aqueous conditions favor SN2; aprotic polar solvents (e.g., DMSO) accelerate SN2 further
alkyl_halide_to_alcohol_naoh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the attacked carbon in the alkyl halide is chiral; SN2 inverts configuration at that center
common_traps: Applying SN2 to tertiary alkyl halides (should be E2 elimination)
Forgetting inversion of configuration for chiral substrates in SN2
Confusing with alc. KOH conditions (which give elimination, not substitution)
alkyl_halide_to_alcohol_naoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SN2 vs E2 based on substrate class); Structural (stereochemistry inversion for chiral centers)

genuine_levers: Zn/H⁺ (or Zn/HCl) reduces alkyl halide to alkane; LiAlH₄ (dry ether) is an alternative strong reducing agent for the same transformation
For gem-dihalides, Zn/H⁺ can give alkene (via double dehalogenation) instead of alkane — substrate structure is a lever
common_traps: Confusing Zn/H⁺ (reduction to alkane) with Zn/Cu couple (Simmons-Smith cyclopropanation) or Zn dust (Reformatsky reaction)
Assuming Zn/H⁺ works on aryl halides (it does NOT; aryl halides are inert to Zn/H⁺ reduction)
alkyl_halide_to_alkane_zn.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: Zn/H⁺ vs Zn/Cu vs LiAlH₄; substrate scope: gem-dihalides give alkene)

genuine_levers: NaI in dry acetone is an SN2 reaction — inversion of configuration at chiral centers
Primary halides react fastest; tertiary halides may not react (E2 with base or SN1 with nucleophile can compete)
The reaction is driven by precipitation of NaCl/NaBr — if solvent is not dry acetone, equilibrium may not shift
alkyl_halide_to_alkyl_halide_nai.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting alkyl halide is chiral; SN2 inverts configuration, so a new stereocenter is not created, but the existing one is inverted
common_traps: Assuming this is an SN1 reaction — it is not; NaI is a good nucleophile in aprotic solvent, favoring SN2
Forgetting that tertiary halides give elimination (E2) with NaI in acetone, not substitution
Mistaking this for Finkelstein reaction with KI in acetone — NaI is used similarly but note the sodium salt precipitation
alkyl_halide_to_alkyl_halide_nai.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SN2 vs SN1/E2; stereochemistry inversion)

genuine_levers: Requires a 1,1,2,2-tetrahalide (geminal dihalides on two adjacent carbons) as the substrate; vicinal dihalides (1,2-dihalides) do NOT give alkynes with Zn
Zn must be in excess (2 equivalents) and heated to drive the double dehalogenation
common_traps: Confusing this with the elimination of a simple vicinal dihalide (which gives an alkene, not an alkyne)
Assuming any dihalide with halogens on adjacent carbons will work (only the tetrahalide works)
Forgetting the heat requirement for the reaction to proceed
vicinal_dihalide_to_alkyne_zndeltaexces.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate recognition: geminal vs. vicinal dihalides)

genuine_levers: Excess NH₃ favours primary amine; limited NH₃ leads to mixture of 1°, 2°, 3° amines and quaternary ammonium salt
Gabriel phthalimide synthesis gives pure primary amine, bypassing polyalkylation
SN2 mechanism: primary alkyl halides preferred; secondary gives elimination, tertiary fails
alkyl_halide_to_amine_excessnh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alkyl halide is chiral and the reaction proceeds via SN2 (inversion of configuration); if the halide is achiral, no stereocenters are created
common_traps: Forgetting that R-NH₂ is nucleophilic and can react with another R-X, leading to polyalkylation unless NH₃ is in excess
Applying this SN2 route to aryl/vinyl halides (which do not undergo SN2)
Confusing this with Gabriel synthesis, which is a distinct route for pure 1° amines
alkyl_halide_to_amine_excessnh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent excess vs. stoichiometry; mechanism choice SN2 vs. elimination; alternative Gabriel synthesis)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice (Mg vs Zn) determines mechanism: Mg → Grignard (carbanion equivalent); Zn → Simmons-Smith carbenoid (cyclopropanation)
Chain length (n) in 1,n-dihaloalkane determines product: n=3-6 → cycloalkane; n=7+ → polymerization (intramolecular cyclization disfavored)
common_traps: Assuming 1,7-dihaloalkane gives a 7-membered ring — it polymerizes instead
Confusing Grignard formation (carbanion) with carbene formation (cyclopropanation) — reagent is key
Overlooking that 'any carbanion conditions' is a placeholder; specific reagent dictates product
alkyl_halide_to_carbanion_mgdelta.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent mechanism discrimination: carbanion vs carbenoid); Structural (chain-length effect on intramolecular vs intermolecular reaction)

genuine_levers: Base size (small base like alc. KOH → Saytzeff; bulky base like t-BuOK → Hofmann)
Leaving group ability (good LG like I⁻ → Saytzeff; poor LG like F⁻ or NR₃⁺ → Hofmann)
Substrate geometry (acyclic → Saytzeff; rigid cyclic → anti-periplanar geometry overrides Saytzeff)
Resonance stabilization of a possible product (conjugated alkene → major, overriding substitution level)
common_traps: Assuming E2 always gives the more substituted (Saytzeff) alkene, ignoring anti-periplanar constraints in cyclic systems
Confusing alc. KOH (E2) with aq. KOH (SN2)
Thinking E1 gives a mixture of regioisomers (it gives only Saytzeff)
Mistaking t-BuOH (solvent, E1) for t-BuO⁻ (base, E2)
Believing fluoride is a good leaving group in E2 (it is poor, favoring Hofmann)
alkyl_halide_to_carbocation_alckohdeltae.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (base/leaving group selectivity; anti-periplanar vs. thermodynamic control)

genuine_levers: Ring size (n) in α,ω-dihaloalkane X-(CH₂)n-X determines cycloalkane product (n=3 → cyclopropane, n=4 → cyclobutane, etc.)
Choice of metal (Mg vs Zn) — both give intramolecular coupling to cycloalkane, but Zn is less common
common_traps: Assuming the product is a linear Grignard dimer (BrMg-(CH₂)₃-MgBr) instead of the intramolecular cyclization product
Forgetting the mechanism: Mg inserts into one C-Br to form a Grignard, which then performs an intramolecular SN2 on the other C-Br
alkyl_halide_to_cycloalkane_mgmagnesiumm.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing intramolecular cyclization vs. standard Grignard formation)

genuine_levers: NaOEt (strong base/nucleophile) in anhydrous ethanol with heat drives SN2 substitution, not elimination; using a bulky base (e.g., t-BuOK) would favor E2 elimination to alkene
CHCl₃ is the specific substrate; using a different alkyl halide (e.g., R-CH₂-Cl) would give a simple ether (R-OEt) via SN2, not an orthoester
common_traps: Mistaking NaOEt/heat for E2 elimination conditions (which would give alkene) — here it's an SN2 substitution on a polyhalogenated substrate
Forgetting the triple substitution on CHCl₃ to form the orthoester HC(OEt)₃, not a mono-substituted product
alkyl_halide_to_ester_naoet.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent role discrimination: NaOEt as nucleophile for SN2 vs. base for E2; substrate-specific triple substitution)

genuine_levers: Primary alkyl halide → SN2 ether; secondary/tertiary halide → E2 alkene — substrate structure is a genuine lever
R'ONa (strong base/nucleophile) → SN2 ether; R'OH/H⁺ (acidic conditions) → SN1 ether (with possible carbocation rearrangement) — reagent identity is a genuine lever
alkyl_halide_to_ether_rona.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the SN2 reaction occurs at a chiral sp3 carbon (inversion of configuration); if the starting alkyl halide is achiral, no new stereocenters are created
common_traps: Using secondary/tertiary alkyl halide leads to E2 elimination (alkene) instead of SN2 ether — a classic mistake
Using aqueous conditions or protic solvent leads to side reactions (hydrolysis to alcohol) — dry aprotic solvent is essential for alkoxide nucleophile
alkyl_halide_to_ether_rona.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate vs. reagent selectivity: SN2 vs. E2; primary vs. secondary/tertiary halide)

genuine_levers: Substrate scope: only activated (benzylic or allylic) alkyl halides react; general alkyl halides give no reaction
Product selectivity: primary halides give aldehydes; secondary halides (if activated) give ketones
common_traps: Applying Kornblum oxidation to a non-activated (simple alkyl) halide — it will not work
Forgetting that DMSO is the oxidant and writing a mechanism involving a carbocation (it's an SN2-like displacement on sulfur)
alkyl_halide_to_ketone_dmsodimethyl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate activation requirement: benzylic/allylic vs general alkyl halides)

genuine_levers: NaCN in polar aprotic solvent (DMSO/DMF) → SN2 → nitrile; in protic solvent → SN1/E1 mix possible; N-attack (isocyanide) is minor but a competing pathway
Primary/secondary alkyl halides → SN2 nitrile; tertiary → E2 elimination (alkene) due to sterics
SN2 mechanism → inversion of configuration at chiral carbon (if present)
alkyl_halide_to_nitrile_nacn.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting alkyl halide is chiral; SN2 inverts configuration, but does not create a new stereocenter from an achiral substrate
common_traps: Confusing NaCN (nitrile product) with KCN/AgCN (major isocyanide product) — reagent identity matters
Forgetting SN2 requires primary/secondary substrate; using tertiary halide gives E2 alkene, not nitrile
Mistaking product as carboxylic acid (requires further hydrolysis) — nitrile is the direct product here
alkyl_halide_to_nitrile_nacn.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent/solvent selectivity: SN2 vs E2, C-attack vs N-attack); Structural (homologation: chain length increases by 1C)

genuine_levers: Ambident nucleophile AgNO2: Ag occupies O-end, N is free donor → N-attack yields R-NO2 (nitro compound). NaNO2 (sodium nitrite) would give O-attack → R-ONO (nitrite ester).
common_traps: Confusing AgNO2 with NaNO2 — NaNO2 gives nitrite ester (R-ONO) via O-attack, not nitro compound.
Assuming AgNO2 attacks via oxygen because Ag is bonded to O — the Ag-O bond locks O, freeing N for nucleophilic attack.
alkyl_halide_to_nitro_compound_agno2silv.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (ambident nucleophile selectivity: AgNO2 vs NaNO2)

genuine_levers: NaNH₂ deprotonates only terminal alkynes (pKa≈25) — internal alkynes (no terminal H) do NOT react
Solvent choice: liquid NH₃ (protic) vs THF (aprotic) — both work, but NH₃ is the classic solvent for this reaction
common_traps: Assuming NaNH₂ reacts with internal alkynes — it does not; only terminal C-H is acidic enough
Forgetting that acetylide ion is a strong nucleophile for SN2 with primary alkyl halides (not secondary/tertiary)
Confusing NaNH₂ with Na (metal) — Na reacts with alcohols/phenols to give H₂; NaNH₂ is a base for deprotonation
alkyne_to_acetylide_ion_nanh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: terminal vs internal alkyne; pKa comparison)

genuine_levers: O3/Zn cleaves triple bond to give carboxylic acids; alternative hydration (Hg2+/H2SO4) gives ketone — reagent choice is a genuine lever
Internal alkyne (R-C≡C-R') yields two different carboxylic acids; terminal alkyne (R-C≡CH) yields R-COOH + HCOOH (formic acid) — substrate structure is a genuine lever
common_traps: Confusing ozonolysis (O3/Zn) with hydration (Hg2+/H2SO4) — the latter gives ketone, not carboxylic acid
Forgetting that terminal alkynes give formic acid (HCOOH) as one product
Mistaking reductive (Zn) for oxidative (H2O2) workup — oxidative workup on alkene gives ketones/aldehydes, but on alkyne still gives carboxylic acids
alkyne_to_alkane_o3.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: O3/Zn vs Hg2+/H2SO4); Structural (predicting products from internal vs terminal alkyne)

genuine_levers: Catalyst choice: Lindlar's (Pd poisoned) gives cis-alkene (Z); Na/NH₃(l) gives trans-alkene (E) — this is a genuine lever
Stopping point: Lindlar's stops at alkene; full hydrogenation (Pd/C, PtO₂) gives alkane — reagent choice controls reduction degree
common_traps: Lindlar's gives Z-alkene; Na/NH₃ gives E-alkene — confusing these is a classic trap
Assuming any H₂/Pd gives alkane — forgetting the poisoned catalyst stops at alkene
alkyne_to_alkene_h.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (catalyst selectivity: Lindlar's vs Na/NH₃ vs Pd/C for alkyne reduction)

genuine_levers: Terminal alkyne (R-C≡C-H) gives R-COOH + CO2 (not R'-COOH) — internal alkyne gives two carboxylic acids
Oxidative workup (H2O2) vs reductive workup (Zn/H2O) — ozonolysis with reductive workup gives diketones, not acids
common_traps: Forgetting that terminal alkyne carbon (C≡C-H) oxidises to CO2 + H2O, not a carboxylic acid
Confusing with alkene cleavage (alkene gives aldehydes/ketones, not acids, unless with hot KMnO4)
Assuming ozonolysis of alkynes always gives acids (requires oxidative workup, H2O2)
alkyne_to_carboxylic_acid_hotkmno4acid.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (terminal vs internal alkyne distinction; oxidative vs reductive workup choice)

genuine_levers: Excess HX (2 equiv) gives geminal dihalide; 1 equiv HX stops at Markovnikov vinyl halide
Reagent choice: HX (excess) vs X2 (excess) — HX gives gem-dihalide, X2 gives 1,1,2,2-tetrahaloalkane
common_traps: Assuming anti-Markovnikov addition for either step (e.g., peroxide effect) — HX addition to alkynes is Markovnikov
Forgetting the second addition to the vinyl halide intermediate, stopping at the monohalide product
alkyne_to_geminal_dihalide_hxexcess2equ.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent stoichiometry and identity: 1 vs 2 equiv, HX vs X2)

genuine_levers: Markovnikov regiochemistry — H adds to terminal carbon, X to internal carbon; anti-Markovnikov addition (e.g., with radical initiators) gives opposite regioisomer
Rate order HI > HBr > HCl — reactivity of HX is a genuine lever for predicting reaction speed or completion
1 equivalent of HX is specified; excess HX leads to geminal dihalide product
alkyne_to_vinyl_halide_hx1equiv.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the resulting alkene is trisubstituted or tetrasubstituted, creating a stereogenic center; terminal alkyne with HX yields a vinyl halide that is often not chiral (e.g., 1-halo-1-alkene from terminal alkyne is not chiral unless the other substituent is not H)
common_traps: Assuming syn addition — HX addition to alkynes is typically anti addition via vinyl cation, leading to mixture of E/Z vinyl halides if internal
Forgetting Markovnikov rule — incorrectly placing X on terminal carbon of terminal alkyne
Using aqueous conditions — HX (g) or neat is required; aqueous HX may lead to hydration side products
alkyne_to_vinyl_halide_hx1equiv.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry control via Markovnikov vs anti-Markovnikov conditions); Structural (if stereocenters form)

genuine_levers: LAH reduces amide to amine; NaBH4 does NOT reduce amides — reagent choice is a genuine lever
LAH reduces amide C=O to CH2, giving a primary amine; other reductants (e.g., catalytic hydrogenation) may give different products or require harsher conditions
amide_to_amine_lah.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting amide nitrogen is chiral (e.g., N-substituted with a chiral group) and stereochemistry is retained; the newly formed CH2 is not a stereocenter
common_traps: NaBH4 is often incorrectly assumed to reduce amides — it does not; LAH is required
Forgetting the aqueous workup step after LAH, which is essential to quench the intermediate alkoxide and liberate the amine
Mistaking the product as an aldehyde or nitrile instead of a primary amine
amide_to_amine_lah.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reductant selectivity: LAH vs NaBH4 vs other reducing agents)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: NaNO2/dil. HCl at 0-5°C → diazonium salt → alcohol via SN1-type substitution with water; different reagents (e.g., NaNO2/HBF4 → Sandmeyer) give different products (e.g., F, Cl, Br, I, CN)
amine_to_alcohol_nano.creates_stereocenters: conditional — if the diazonium group is replaced at a chiral sp3 carbon, inversion occurs (SN2-like via diazonium loss), potentially creating a stereocenter; but the common aromatic amine → phenol case does not create stereocenters
common_traps: Confusing with nitrous acid test for amines: 1° aliphatic amine → alcohol via diazonium, but 1° aromatic amine → phenol; 2° amine → N-nitrosamine (yellow oil); 3° amine → salt (no N2 evolution)
Forgetting the temperature (0-5°C) leads to diazonium decomposition and side products (phenols can couple at higher temps)
Mistaking this for a simple substitution — it's a two-step diazotization-aqueous substitution sequence
amine_to_alcohol_nano.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and condition specificity for diazotization vs. other amine reactions); Structural if the substrate is aliphatic and chiral (stereochemistry)

genuine_levers: Oxidant choice: KMnO4 (acidic, hot) leads to over-oxidation to carboxylic acid; milder oxidants (e.g., CuO, 300°C) give nitrile
Substrate type: Primary amines (R-CH2-NH2) can be oxidized; tertiary amines are inert to KMnO4 and require peracids to form amine oxides
common_traps: Assuming KMnO4 oxidation of a primary amine stops at aldehyde (it typically over-oxidizes to carboxylic acid)
Applying this transformation to tertiary amines (they do not react with KMnO4 under these conditions)
amine_to_aldehyde_kmno4overoxi.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant and substrate selectivity; exception recognition for over-oxidation)

genuine_levers: Primary vs. secondary amine determines amide substitution (monosubstituted vs. disubstituted)
Acyl chloride (RCOCl) is the archetypal reagent; using acyl anhydride gives slower reaction and acid byproduct; using DCC couples carboxylic acid directly to amine
amine_to_amide_rcocl.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the amine nitrogen is chiral (rare) or if the R group of the acyl chloride introduces a new stereocenter
common_traps: Omitting the base (pyridine/Et₃N) leads to HCl salt formation and incomplete reaction
Confusing this with amide hydrolysis (amide → amine) under acidic/basic conditions
amine_to_amide_rcocl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: acyl chloride vs. anhydride vs. DCC coupling)

genuine_levers: Excess NH3 favors primary amine (monoalkylation); excess alkyl halide drives exhaustive methylation to quaternary ammonium salt
Gabriel synthesis is a specific route to pure primary amines, avoiding mixtures from direct alkylation
Hofmann exhaustive methylation (CH3I excess → Ag2O/H2O → heat) leads to elimination, not further alkylation
amine_to_amine_heatsn2.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alkyl halide is chiral and the amine nitrogen becomes a stereocenter upon quaternization (chiral ammonium salt)
common_traps: Assuming direct alkylation gives a pure primary amine product (it yields a mixture of 1°, 2°, 3°, and 4° amines)
Using Gabriel synthesis with an aryl halide (ArX does not undergo SN2)
Confusing exhaustive alkylation (to quaternary salt) with Hofmann elimination (which follows quaternization and Ag2O treatment)
amine_to_amine_heatsn2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (controlling product distribution via reagent excess; distinguishing Gabriel from direct alkylation)

genuine_levers: Dry heat (200°C) is essential — aqueous acid conditions would lead to different products (e.g., diazonium chemistry)
The rearrangement proceeds via an alkyl cation — the identity of the N-alkyl group determines the electrophile that attacks the ring
amine_to_aniline_heat200chofm.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the migrating alkyl group is chiral; the resulting C-alkylation at the ortho/para position creates a new stereocenter, but racemization of the alkyl cation is likely
common_traps: Assuming this is a simple acid-catalyzed alkylation — it is a thermal rearrangement of the salt
Forgetting that the product is a mixture of ortho- and para-alkylanilines
Confusing this with the Hofmann degradation (amide to amine) due to the name 'Hofmann'
amine_to_aniline_heat200chofm.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism recognition: thermal rearrangement vs. typical electrophilic aromatic substitution conditions)

genuine_levers: Temperature control (0-5°C) is critical for stable diazonium salt; >5°C leads to decomposition to phenol
Substrate specificity: only primary aromatic amines react; aliphatic amines give unstable diazonium that decomposes to carbocation
common_traps: Assuming aliphatic diazonium salts are stable and can be used like aromatic ones (they decompose instantly)
Forgetting the temperature condition and predicting a stable diazonium salt at room temperature
amine_to_diazonium_salt_nano.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate and condition specificity: aromatic vs aliphatic, temperature control)

genuine_levers: Primary amine + aldehyde gives imine; 2° amine + aldehyde gives enamine — substrate identity is a genuine lever
Reaction is reversible; removal of water (Dean-Stark, molecular sieves) drives equilibrium to product — condition choice is a genuine lever
amine_to_imine_rcho.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting carbonyl is a ketone (R≠H) and the resulting imine carbon bears four different groups (R, R', N-substituent, lone pair); check substitution before asserting chirality
common_traps: Writing imine formation for a secondary amine (correct product is enamine)
Forgetting the acid catalysis (pH 4–5) or the dehydration step (–H₂O)
Assuming the reaction is irreversible; acid hydrolysis regenerates starting materials
amine_to_imine_rcho.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: 1° vs 2° amine; equilibrium control via water removal)

genuine_levers: Oxidant choice: KMnO4 oxidizes primary amines to nitro compounds; mCPBA oxidizes tertiary amines to N-oxides
Substrate class: Primary amines (R-NH2) give nitro compounds; secondary amines (R2NH) give N-nitrosoamines or hydroxylamines; tertiary amines (R3N) are inert to KMnO4
common_traps: Assuming KMnO4 oxidizes tertiary amines (it does not — use peracids for N-oxides)
Confusing this with over-oxidation of primary alcohols to carboxylic acids (KMnO4 stops at aldehyde only under specific acidic conditions, which is an exception)
amine_to_nitro_compound_kmno4.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: primary vs secondary vs tertiary amine behavior with different oxidants)

genuine_levers: Priority assignment for COOH vs CH2OH using Rule 4 phantom atoms (COOH-C bonded to O,O,OH) vs CH2OH-C bonded to O,H,H)
Priority assignment for -CH2SH vs COOH in cysteine (S > O, so -CH2SH > COOH, flipping configuration from (S) to (R))
common_traps: Assuming all L-amino acids are (S) configuration (L-cysteine is (R))
Incorrectly assigning priority to COOH without using phantom atoms for the carbonyl oxygen (Rule 4)
Mistaking Fischer projection 'left/right' for CIP 'clockwise/counterclockwise' without proper 3D model manipulation
amino_acid_to_amino_acid_cipsequencer.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (CIP sequence rule application, especially Rule 4 phantom atoms for carbonyls and heteroatom priority)

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 (excess) reduces BOTH carbonyls of the anhydride to primary alcohols, giving a diol; NaBH4 is also effective but less common; BH3 can give lactol intermediates
Cyclic anhydride structure dictates the 1,4-diol product (e.g., succinic anhydride → butane-1,4-diol); acyclic anhydrides give two separate alcohol chains
common_traps: Mistaking LiAlH4 reduction for hydrolysis (aqueous acid/base hydrolysis gives diacid, not diol)
Forgetting the excess reagent requirement (1 equivalent reduces only one carbonyl, giving a hydroxyaldehyde or hydroxyacid)
anhydride_to_alkane_1lialh4exces.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: LiAlH4/NaBH4 reduction vs. hydrolysis; understanding anhydride reactivity vs. esters)

genuine_levers: AlCl₃ is a Lewis acid catalyst for Friedel-Crafts acylation; if the substrate is an aromatic ether, the acylium attacks the aromatic ring (electrophilic aromatic substitution) leading to aryl ketone, not ester formation

The described transformation (anhydride to ester) specifically requires cleavage of an ether (e.g., diaryl ether) by the acylating agent; if the substrate is a simple alcohol, the product is a different ester via nucleophilic acyl substitution
anhydride_to_ester_alcllewisaci.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the resulting ester's α-carbon becomes chiral; typically not, unless the acyl group or the alcohol moiety introduces a new stereocenter
common_traps: Mistaking this for simple esterification (anhydride + alcohol → ester + acid); here, the ether is cleaved, and both C-O bonds are broken
Assuming AlCl₃ always gives Friedel-Crafts alkylation/acylation on arenes; in this specific case, it activates the anhydride to cleave an ether linkage
anhydride_to_ester_alcllewisaci.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: Friedel-Crafts acylation vs. ether cleavage by acylating agent)

genuine_levers: Strict temperature control (0-5°C) is essential to prevent decomposition of the diazonium salt to phenol
The amine must be primary aromatic (Ar-NH2); secondary amines yield N-nitrosamines, aliphatic primary amines yield unstable alkyl diazonium salts that decompose to carbocations
common_traps: Confusing the stability of aromatic diazonium salts (Ar-N2+) with the extreme instability of aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N2+)
Forgetting that aniline must be protonated (as anilinium ion) for the reaction to proceed, which temporarily makes the ring meta-directing for any concurrent EAS
Assuming the reaction works for any primary amine, including alkyl amines, which instead yield alcohols via carbocation intermediates
aniline_to_diazonium_salt_nano2hclat05.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate specificity: aromatic vs. aliphatic primary amines; temperature control)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice determines product: KMnO4/H+ or K2Cr2O7/H+ gives p-benzoquinone; HNO3/heat can lead to complex oxidation/nitration mixtures; basic conditions (e.g., H2O2/OH-) may give azobenzene derivatives.
common_traps: Assuming aniline oxidation gives a simple ketone like acetophenone (it gives p-benzoquinone, a cyclic diketone).
Forgetting that acidic conditions are crucial for quinone formation; basic conditions give different products.
Overlooking that this is a 6-electron oxidation per molecule, not a simple functional group interconversion.
aniline_to_ketone_kmno4heatorh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and condition specificity for aromatic amine oxidation)

genuine_levers: Caro's acid (H2SO5) oxidises aniline to nitrobenzene via hydroxylamine intermediate; other oxidants (KMnO4, CrO3) give different products or fail
Regiochemistry of EAS on the substituted benzene product is determined by the nitro group (meta-director) and any other substituents present
common_traps: Assuming aniline is oxidised to nitrobenzene by common oxidants like KMnO4 (it is not; KMnO4 oxidises alkyl side chains, not the amine directly to nitro)
Forgetting that the nitro product is a meta-director for subsequent EAS, unlike the starting aniline which is ortho/para
Applying aniline's ortho/para directing rules to the nitrobenzene product in a follow-up step
aniline_to_substituted_benzene_carosacid.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant specificity: Caro's acid vs. common oxidants); Structural (regiochemistry shift from EDG to EWG after oxidation)

genuine_levers: Halide reactivity order for SNAr is F > Cl > Br > I (opposite to SN2/SN1) — choice of halide is a genuine lever for reaction rate
Strong electron-withdrawing groups ortho/para to the halide accelerate SNAr by stabilizing the Meisenheimer complex
Strong electron-donating groups ortho/para to the halide inhibit SNAr
Choice of nucleophile (Nu:-) determines the final substituent on the aromatic ring
common_traps: Applying SN2/SN1 halide reactivity order (I > Br > Cl > F) to SNAr is a classic mistake
Assuming the reaction proceeds via a carbocation intermediate (SN1) instead of addition-elimination (SNAr)
Forgetting that the nucleophile attacks the ipso carbon bearing the leaving group, not a different position on the ring
aryl_halide_to_aryl_halide_anynu.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SNAr vs SN2/SN1; understanding halide reactivity reversal)

genuine_levers: Aryl halides require THF (not Et₂O) for Grignard formation — solvent choice is a genuine lever
Ortho substituents (e.g., OMe) slow formation due to sterics but do NOT prevent it — reactivity lever
Contrast with ortho-bromophenol: acidic OH leads to self-destruction (gives O-MgBr), while OMe is safe — substrate functional group is a genuine lever
common_traps: Assuming aryl halides do not form Grignards (they do, but require THF and heat)
Assuming ortho-OMe blocks Grignard formation entirely (it slows but works)
Confusing ortho-OMe with ortho-OH (phenol) — the latter self-destructs, the former does not
aryl_halide_to_grignard_mgmagnesiumm.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate and solvent selectivity for aryl Grignard formation)

genuine_levers: Harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm) required for unactivated aryl halides — milder conditions (NaNH₂, NH₃(l)) suffice for activated rings with ortho-EWGs
Benzyne mechanism (E2-like elimination → aryne → addition) — different from typical SNAr; regiochemistry of addition determined by substituents on the aryne ring
common_traps: Assuming aryl halides undergo standard SN2 or SNAr (Meisenheimer) under mild conditions — they do not; requires benzyne pathway
Forgetting the acid workup (H⁺) to convert sodium phenoxide to phenol in Dow's process
Applying this reaction to alkyl halides — this is specific to aryl halides
aryl_halide_to_phenol_naoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism shift to benzyne; harsh conditions vs. typical nucleophilic substitution)

genuine_levers: Strong electron-withdrawing group(s) ortho/para to leaving group is essential for SNAr (addition-elimination) — no EWG → no reaction via this mechanism
Choice of nucleophile determines the substituent introduced (e.g., NaOMe gives OMe, NaCN gives CN, RNH2 gives NHR)
Benzyne mechanism (elimination-addition) competes if very strong base (e.g., NaNH2) is used with an aryl halide lacking ortho EWG — leads to mixture of regioisomers
common_traps: Applying SN1/SN2 logic to aryl halides — aryl carbocations are unstable, so typical aliphatic substitution mechanisms do not apply
Assuming any nucleophile will work — reaction requires activated aryl halide (with -NO2, -CN, etc. ortho/para to leaving group)
Confusing SNAr with electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) — here the aromatic ring is attacked by a nucleophile, not an electrophile
aryl_halide_to_substituted_benzene_naome.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SNAr addition-elimination vs. benzyne vs. aliphatic substitution; substrate activation requirement)

genuine_levers: Oxirane (epoxide) as electrophile source vs. standard alkyl halide in Friedel-Crafts alkylation — oxirane gives primary alcohol product (2-phenylethanol), alkyl halide gives simple alkylbenzene
AlCl₃ is essential Lewis acid to activate the epoxide; other Lewis acids (FeCl₃, BF₃) may also work, but protic acids (H₂SO₄) would open epoxide differently
common_traps: Confusing with Friedel-Crafts acylation — this is an alkylation, but product is an alcohol, not a ketone
Mistaking oxirane for ethene — benzene + CH₂=CH₂/AlCl₃ gives ethylbenzene (no OH group), not 2-phenylethanol
Forgetting that the electrophile is a primary carbocation equivalent (Ph-CH₂-CH₂⁺), which is stabilized by the adjacent oxygen atom of the opened epoxide ring
benzene_to_alcohol_oxirane.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing epoxide as a unique electrophile source in Friedel-Crafts alkylation to install a -CH₂CH₂OH group)

genuine_levers: Requires CO + HCl + AlCl₃/CuCl (both Lewis acids) — AlCl₃ alone insufficient; CuCl stabilizes CO complex
Only works for unactivated benzene (e.g., benzene, toluene) — fails for strongly activated rings (phenol, aniline) which undergo Duff reaction instead
Uses formyl cation (H–C≡O⁺) as electrophile — distinct from Friedel-Crafts acylation which uses R–C≡O⁺
common_traps: Confusing with Friedel-Crafts acylation (R–C≡O⁺ → ketone) — this reaction gives aldehyde directly
Assuming AlCl₃ alone is sufficient — CuCl co-catalyst is essential for generating the formyl cation
Applying to phenol/aniline — these activated substrates do NOT give benzaldehyde via this method
benzene_to_aldehyde_co.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent specificity: AlCl₃/CuCl vs AlCl₃ alone; substrate scope: unactivated vs activated arenes)

genuine_levers: Radical addition (UV/high temp) vs electrophilic aromatic substitution (Cl2/FeCl3) — mechanism choice determines product: addition gives BHC, substitution gives chlorobenzene
common_traps: Confusing this with electrophilic chlorination (Cl2/FeCl3) to give chlorobenzene
Forgetting that the product is a cyclohexane derivative (C6H6Cl6), not a substituted benzene
Assuming the reaction is stereospecific — it yields a mixture of stereoisomers, not a single defined product
benzene_to_alkyl_halide_3cl2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: radical addition vs electrophilic substitution)

genuine_levers: Choice of halogen (X₂) and matching Lewis acid catalyst (FeCl₃ for Cl₂, FeBr₃/AlCl₃ for Br₂) — iodination requires ICl or I₂/HNO₃
Presence of an existing substituent on benzene determines regiochemistry via ortho/para or meta directing effects — a genuine lever for product prediction
Highly activated rings (phenol, aniline) react without a Lewis acid catalyst and give polyhalogenated products — catalyst requirement is a lever
common_traps: Assuming iodination works with I₂ alone (it's too slow; requires ICl or oxidant)
Forgetting that aniline forms Ph-NH₃⁺ in acidic conditions, switching it from an ortho/para director to a meta director
Applying the catalyst rule universally (phenol/aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without catalyst)
Predicting ortho/para substitution without considering steric hindrance from existing ortho substituents
benzene_to_aryl_halide_x.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry via directing effects, catalyst requirement based on substrate activation)

genuine_levers: Fuming H₂SO₄ (SO₃) gives sulphonation; conc. H₂SO₄ alone is slower/less effective — reagent strength is a lever
Reaction is reversible (hydrolysis at 150–200°C removes SO₃H group) — temperature/conditions determine direction
Regiochemistry is determined by existing substituents (ortho/para vs meta directors) — a classic EAS lever
common_traps: Forgetting that sulphonation is reversible, unlike most other EAS reactions
Using fuming H₂SO₄ for nitration or halogenation (wrong electrophile) — SO₃ is the electrophile here, not NO₂⁺ or Br⁺
Assuming temperature only affects rate, not regiochemistry (e.g., naphthalene sulphonation: α at low T, β at high T)
benzene_to_carboxylic_acid_fuminghsoole.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent/electrophile identity; reversibility; temperature-dependent regiochemistry in fused systems)

genuine_levers: Regiochemistry determined by existing substituents on benzene ring (ortho/para vs meta directors) — a genuine lever for predicting the major product
Choice of R group in RCOCl determines the structure of the final ketone product
Deactivated rings (e.g., with NO₂, COOH) do not react — substrate activity is a genuine lever
common_traps: Confusing Friedel-Crafts acylation with alkylation — acylium ion (RCO⁺) does NOT rearrange, unlike carbocations in alkylation
Forgetting that AlCl₃ is used in >1 equivalent because it complexes with the product ketone
Applying this reaction to strongly deactivated rings (e.g., nitrobenzene) which do not undergo FC acylation
benzene_to_ketone_rcocl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry based on existing substituents); Structural (predicting the ketone structure from R group)

genuine_levers: Fenton's reagent (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂) generates hydroxyl radicals for radical aromatic substitution — alternative hydroxylation methods (e.g., Dow process, cumene process) involve different mechanisms and conditions

Regiochemistry is determined by existing substituents on the benzene ring — the hydroxyl radical is unselective, but the final phenol's OH position is dictated by the substituent's directing effects in the subsequent oxidation step
common_traps: Assuming phenol is prepared industrially via this direct hydroxylation — the cumene process is the major industrial route
Confusing this radical mechanism with standard electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) — no Lewis acid catalyst is involved
Overlooking that phenol itself is highly activated and undergoes further electrophilic substitution (e.g., tribromination) without a catalyst, unlike benzene
benzene_to_phenol_feso.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: radical vs electrophilic aromatic substitution; industrial vs lab synthesis routes)

genuine_levers: Regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s) — ortho/para vs meta director
Temperature control: >60°C leads to dinitration (multiple substitutions)
Choice of electrophile (NO2+) — other electrophiles (e.g., Cl+, RCO+) give different substituted benzenes
common_traps: Assuming nitration always gives meta product (only true if the substituent is meta-directing)
Forgetting that nitrobenzene is a meta-director for further EAS
Using dilute HNO3/H2SO4 instead of concentrated acids leads to poor NO2+ generation and low yield
benzene_to_substituted_benzene_conchno.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry based on existing substituent directors)

genuine_levers: Stability order: benzyl > allyl > sp > sp2 > sp3 — a more stable carbanion is less reactive/nucleophilic
Resonance stabilization by adjacent pi system (allyl, benzyl) or sp hybridization is a genuine lever; hyperconjugation does NOT stabilize carbanions
Electron-withdrawing groups (-M) stabilize carbanions; electron-donating groups (+M) destabilize them
common_traps: Mistaking carbanion stability order for carbocation stability order (carbocation: 3° > 2° > 1°; carbanion: opposite)
Incorrectly applying hyperconjugation to stabilize a carbanion (hyperconjugation stabilizes carbocations, not carbanions)
Confusing the effect of -M/+M groups on carbanion stability vs. carbocation stability
carbanion_to_carbanion_conceptualme.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (stability trends and substituent effects comparison: carbanion vs. carbocation vs. radical)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice is a genuine lever: Mg/ether gives Grignard reagent (C1-MgCl) which can undergo INTERMOLECULAR reactions; Mg/Delta (activated Mg) promotes INTRAMOLECULAR cyclization to cycloalkane
Ring size is determined by the number of carbons between the two halogens in the 1,ω-dihaloalkane substrate (e.g., 1,3-dichloro → cyclopropane, 1,4-dichloro → cyclobutane)
common_traps: Confusing this intramolecular SN2 cyclization (carbanion pathway) with the Wurtz reaction (intermolecular coupling of two alkyl halides with Na) which gives a longer chain alkane
Assuming the product is an alkene (E2 elimination) — this is a cyclization, not an elimination
Forgetting that the attacking carbon must have carbanion/nucleophilic character (via Mg insertion), not a free carbocation
carbanion_to_cycloalkane_mgdelta.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent conditions: Mg/Delta vs Mg/ether vs Na vs alc. KOH dictate cyclization vs Grignard formation vs Wurtz vs elimination)

genuine_levers: KMnO4 oxidizes primary amines to nitro compounds; peracids (mCPBA, H2O2) oxidize tertiary amines to amine oxides — reagent choice is a genuine lever
Substrate matters: 'tertiary primary amine' (R3C-NH2) gives nitro compound; normal primary amine (RCH2NH2) can over-oxidize to carboxylic acid via aldehyde
common_traps: Assuming KMnO4 oxidizes all amines — it does NOT oxidize tertiary amines (R3N)
Confusing this with over-oxidation: for RCH2NH2, KMnO4 can give RCOOH, not RNO2
Mistaking conditions: neutral/mildly basic KMnO4 for amine oxidation; acidic KMnO4 oxidizes alcohols/alkyl side chains
carbanion_to_nitro_compound_kmno4.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and substrate selectivity for amine oxidation; exception handling)

genuine_levers: Base choice (OH- vs EtO-) determines reaction pathway: OH- can lead to elimination via E1cb, EtO- can act as a base for elimination or as a nucleophile for substitution
Solvent choice (EtOD) is a diagnostic lever for carbanion formation — D exchange observed confirms carbanion intermediate
Substrate requirement: β-C must bear an EWG (e.g., Ph group) to stabilize the carbanion; without it, E1cb is not favored
common_traps: Assuming F is a good leaving group in typical SN2/E2 — it is poor, so E1cb is operative here
Confusing E1cb with E1 — E1cb involves carbanion formation before leaving group departure, not carbocation
Overlooking the need for an EWG at the β-position to stabilize the carbanion for E1cb to occur
carbanion_to_substituted_benzene_ohdelta.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: E1cb vs E2 vs SN2; identifying carbanion stabilization by EWGs)

genuine_levers: Generation method: UV for diazomethane vs. heat for ketene determines the specific carbene precursor and its stability/reactivity
Carbene type: Methylene (:CH₂) from diazomethane vs. substituted carbenes from other diazo compounds (e.g., :CCl₂ from CHCl₃ + KOH)
common_traps: Confusing carbene generation (loss of N₂) with elimination reactions (loss of HX)
Assuming all carbenes are generated the same way; diazomethane requires UV, ketene requires heat
Thinking carbene is a stable intermediate; it is highly reactive and undergoes immediate addition/insertion
carbene_to_carbene_uvhvfordiazo.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (distinguishing generation methods for reactive intermediates)

genuine_levers: Equilibrium position depends on carbocation structure: cyclopropylcarbinyl vs. cyclobutyl vs. allyl cation stability
Sigma resonance (Walsh orbital donation) from cyclopropane stabilizes adjacent carbocation more effectively than pi resonance in allyl cation
common_traps: Assuming allyl cation is more stable than cyclopropylcarbinyl cation (it is not; allyl is only 5% in equilibrium mixture)
Forgetting that cyclopropylcarbinyl and cyclobutyl chlorides solvolyze to the same product mixture via common carbocation intermediate
carbocation_to_carbocation_conceptualme.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (understanding non-classical carbocation stabilization via sigma resonance and equilibrium analysis)

genuine_levers: Acetylation of all free OH groups — if fewer equivalents of Ac2O are used, a partially acetylated product forms
Open-chain vs cyclic form: CHO group in open chain does NOT react with Ac2O; in cyclic form, the anomeric OH (C1) IS acetylated
Sucrose (disaccharide) gives octaacetate — number of OH groups is a genuine lever
common_traps: Acetylating the CHO group (aldehyde) — it does NOT react in the open-chain form; only OH groups react
Forgetting that cyclic glucose pentaacetate has no free CHO, so it gives a negative oxime test — a classic confirmatory test
Assuming all carbohydrates have the same number of free OH groups (e.g., glucose has 5, sucrose has 8)
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_ch3co2o.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (distinguishing open-chain vs cyclic reactivity of CHO group; counting free OH groups)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: SOCl₂ vs PCl₅ vs PCl₃ vs (COCl)₂/DMF (Vilsmeier) — all give acid chloride, but reactivity and conditions differ; SOCl₂ is most common with gaseous byproducts driving reaction
Anhydrous conditions are critical — acid chloride product is highly susceptible to hydrolysis back to carboxylic acid
common_traps: SOCl₂ with chiral α‑carbon retains configuration (inversion does NOT occur) — do not assume SN2‑like stereochemistry
Confusing SOCl₂ (for acid chloride) with SOCI₂ for alcohol chlorination (which proceeds with inversion)
Using aqueous conditions or protic solvents leads to hydrolysis of the acid chloride product
carboxylic_acid_to_acid_chloride_socl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selection and condition awareness: anhydrous setup, byproduct evolution, retention of α‑stereochemistry)

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces COOH to primary alcohol via aldehyde intermediate; NaBH4 is too mild and does NOT reduce carboxylic acids
BH3 (borane) also reduces COOH to primary alcohol, but has a different selectivity order (COOH easiest) compared to LiAlH4
carboxylic_acid_to_alcohol_lialh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carboxylic acid carbon is prochiral (i.e., R-COOH where R is a chiral group) and the reduction creates a new chiral alcohol center; otherwise false for simple R-COOH
common_traps: NaBH4 is often incorrectly chosen for carboxylic acid reduction — it is ineffective
Aqueous workup must be careful (LiAlH4 reacts violently with water); ignoring this is a procedural trap
Assuming LiAlH4 reduces COOH to aldehyde (it goes all the way to alcohol; the aldehyde is not isolable)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice determines mechanism and product: Soda-lime (NaOH/CaO) heat gives alkane (R-H); Kolbe electrolysis gives symmetric alkane (R-R); Hunsdiecker (Ag salt + Br2) gives alkyl bromide (R-Br, one carbon shorter)
common_traps: Confusing soda-lime decarboxylation (alkane) with Kolbe electrolysis (dimer alkane)
Applying Kolbe electrolysis to formate (HCOONa) expecting CH4 (does not work)
Forgetting that Kolbe-Schmitt reaction (with phenoxide) gives para-hydroxybenzoic acid, not ortho
carboxylic_acid_to_alkane_naoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent/condition discrimination: decarboxylation vs. electrolysis vs. Hunsdiecker)

genuine_levers: Halogen choice (Br2 vs I2) determines product: Br2 yields alkyl bromide (Hunsdiecker), I2 in 1:1 ratio yields alkyl iodide, I2 in 2:1 ratio yields ester (Simonini reaction) — reagent stoichiometry is a genuine lever
Metal salt choice (Ag+ vs Cu2+) determines mechanism: Ag+ salt leads to radical pathway (Hunsdiecker), Cu2+ salt (Kochi) leads to carbocation pathway and potential rearrangement
carboxylic_acid_to_alkyl_halide_agohtoma.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the reaction proceeds via the Kochi (Cu2+) carbocation pathway and the resulting carbocation is attacked with stereochemical control; the radical Hunsdiecker pathway is stereorandom
common_traps: Forgetting the chain shortening (loss of CO2) and writing a product with the same number of carbons as the acid
Assuming the reaction always gives an alkyl halide — with excess I2, an ester is formed
Applying carbocation rearrangement logic to the radical Hunsdiecker mechanism (Ag+), where it does not occur
carboxylic_acid_to_alkyl_halide_agohtoma.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent/stoichiometry selectivity: Hunsdiecker vs Simonini vs Kochi); Structural (carbocation rearrangement in Kochi variant)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Direct heating with NH3 gives amide; using DCC gives amide under milder conditions; using acid chloride first (via SOCl2) then amine gives amide faster
Amine nucleophile: Using a primary amine (RNH2) instead of NH3 gives a substituted amide (RCONHR')
Temperature: High heat (150–200°C) is required for the direct route; lower temperatures may only give the ammonium salt
carboxylic_acid_to_amide_nh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carboxylic acid alpha-carbon is chiral; the reaction proceeds with retention of configuration at the alpha-carbon (no inversion via the tetrahedral intermediate)
common_traps: Forgetting the high temperature requirement and writing the ammonium carboxylate salt as the final product
Confusing this direct route with the milder, more common acid chloride/amine or DCC coupling methods shown in synthesis
Assuming racemization occurs at a chiral alpha-carbon (it typically does not under these thermal conditions)
carboxylic_acid_to_amide_nh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and condition discrimination: direct thermal vs activated acid routes)

genuine_levers: Reagent identity is critical: HN3/H2SO4 is the Schmidt reaction (acid → amine with loss of CO2). Other reagents (e.g., SOCl2 then NH3) give amide without decarboxylation.
Substrate scope: Schmidt reaction works on carboxylic acids, ketones (→ amide with ring expansion), and alcohols (→ nitrilium). The product class depends on the starting functional group.
carboxylic_acid_to_amine_hn3h2so4.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the resulting amine is chiral (i.e., the R group in R-NH2 is chiral). The reaction proceeds via an achiral isocyanate intermediate, so stereochemistry at the alpha carbon is retained if present.
common_traps: Confusing Schmidt (HN3) with Curtius (acyl azide) rearrangement — both give amines via isocyanate, but Curtius starts from acyl azide (RCON3) derived from acid chloride.
Forgetting the loss of one carbon atom (CO2 evolution) — product amine has one fewer carbon than the starting acid.
Mistaking the product for an amide (RCONH2) instead of the primary amine (RNH2).
carboxylic_acid_to_amine_hn3h2so4.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent-driven mechanism discrimination: Schmidt vs. other acid-to-amine conversions); Structural (carbon count change)

genuine_levers: Intramolecular cyclization requires 1,2- or 1,4-diacid proximity; 1,3- or 1,5-diacids do not form 5- or 6-membered anhydrides under these conditions
Heat alone (Δ) is sufficient for ortho-diacids; acid catalyst (H₂SO₄) is needed for less reactive or non-ortho diacids
common_traps: Applying this reaction to any dicarboxylic acid (e.g., 1,3-diacid) — it only works for specific relative positions
Assuming an intermolecular esterification product (linear polymer) instead of the intramolecular cyclic anhydride

genuine_levers: NaOH + CaO (soda lime) + Δ → decarboxylation to arene; Cu powder (Ullmann) → biaryl coupling; Pd catalyst → decarboxylative cross-coupling
common_traps: Applying soda-lime decarboxylation to aliphatic carboxylic acids (only works for aromatic acids or β-keto acids)
Confusing with Kolbe electrolysis (which gives alkane dimer) or Hunsdiecker reaction (which gives alkyl halide)
carboxylic_acid_to_benzene_naoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent choice: soda lime vs Cu vs Pd determines product)

genuine_levers: Requires α-H — no reaction for carboxylic acids lacking α-hydrogen (e.g., 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid)
Halogen identity (X₂ = Cl₂ or Br₂) determines α-halo acid product; I₂ is not used in HVZ
