Catalytic P (red or white) is essential to generate PX₃ in situ; reaction fails without it
carboxylic_acid_to_carboxylic_acid_x.creates_stereocenters: conditional — if the α-carbon is prochiral, halogenation creates a chiral α-carbon; product is racemic due to enol intermediate
common_traps: HVZ is ONLY for α-halogenation of carboxylic acids; applying it to aldehydes/ketones (α-halogenation via acid/base catalysis) is a different mechanism
Confusing HVZ with electrophilic aromatic halogenation (requires Lewis acid, not P)
Assuming the product is an acyl halide (RCOX) — the final product is the α-halo carboxylic acid
carboxylic_acid_to_carboxylic_acid_x.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent role: P catalyst to form PX₃; mechanism via enolisation of acyl halide intermediate)

genuine_levers: Equilibrium reaction — yield driven by excess ROH or removal of H₂O (Dean-Stark trap)
Sterically hindered acids (e.g., 2,6-disubstituted) give poor yields — alternative methods (DCC coupling, acyl chloride) required
common_traps: Assuming the reaction is irreversible — it is an equilibrium; driving force must be specified
Confusing Fischer esterification (acid-catalyzed, reversible) with esterification via acyl chloride (irreversible, base workup)
carboxylic_acid_to_ester_roh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (equilibrium control vs. irreversible methods; steric hindrance as a lever)

genuine_levers: Carbene (:CH₂) from CH₂N₂/UV inserts into C–H bonds (major product: methylcyclopentane) OR into C–C bonds (ring expansion product: cyclohexane) — product mixture is a genuine lever
Ring strain drives C–C insertion for small rings (cyclopropane, cyclobutane) — ring size is a genuine lever
cycloalkane_to_cycloalkane_diazomethane.creates_stereocenters: conditional — if the carbene inserts into a C–H bond on a chiral cycloalkane, a new stereocenter is created; for C–C insertion (ring expansion), stereocenters are scrambled via carbene rearrangement
common_traps: Assuming only ring expansion occurs — C–H insertion is a major competing pathway
Forgetting that diazomethane is explosive and toxic — a safety note, not a mechanistic trap
Mistaking the carbene intermediate for a carbocation — insertion is concerted, not ionic
cycloalkane_to_cycloalkane_diazomethane.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (product distribution: C–H vs C–C insertion selectivity based on ring size and conditions)

genuine_levers: Reductive workup (Zn/H2O or DMS) yields aldehydes; oxidative workup (H2O2) yields carboxylic acids — workup choice is a genuine lever
Cyclic alkene yields a single bifunctional product (dialdehyde) upon ring opening; acyclic alkene yields two separate carbonyl fragments
common_traps: Mistaking the product for two separate aldehyde molecules from a cyclic alkene (it is one dialdehyde)
Using oxidative workup (H2O2) and incorrectly predicting aldehydes instead of acids
Forgetting that ozonolysis of alkenes cleaves the C=C double bond completely
cycloalkene_to_alkane_o3.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (workup selectivity: reductive vs oxidative); Structural (cyclic vs acyclic substrate determines product connectivity)

genuine_levers: Aliphatic vs aromatic diazonium stability — aliphatic R-N₂⁺ instantly loses N₂ to carbocation; aromatic Ar-N₂⁺ is stable at 0–5°C and reacts via different pathways (Sandmeyer, azo coupling)
Nucleophile choice — water gives alcohol; other nucleophiles (halide, CN⁻, I⁻, etc.) give different substitution products (alkyl halide, nitrile, etc.)
diazonium_salt_to_alcohol_howaterasnuc.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carbocation intermediate is planar and the resulting sp³ carbon bears four different groups after nucleophilic attack; rearrangement can alter the carbon skeleton
common_traps: Assuming aromatic diazonium salt (Ar-N₂⁺) hydrolyses via the same SN1 carbocation mechanism as aliphatic — it does not; Ar⁺ is too unstable
Forgetting carbocation rearrangements (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shifts) when a more stable cation is accessible
Mistaking this for an SN2 substitution — back-side attack is impossible on the planar carbocation intermediate
diazonium_salt_to_alcohol_howaterasnuc.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (aliphatic vs aromatic diazonium reactivity dichotomy); Structural (carbocation rearrangement possibilities)

genuine_levers: Choice of CuX determines halogen (X = Cl, Br, CN) in Sandmeyer; KI/heat for ArI (no Cu catalyst) is a distinct lever
Balz-Schiemann (HBF₄/Δ) for ArF is a separate reagent lever
Gattermann (Cu powder) vs. Sandmeyer (CuX) for ArCl/ArBr is a milder alternative lever
common_traps: Assuming aliphatic diazonium salts are stable and can undergo Sandmeyer (they decompose instantly to carbocations)
Applying Sandmeyer CuX rule to ArI synthesis (ArI uses KI/heat directly, no Cu catalyst)
Forgetting that diazonium salt stability requires 0–5°C temperature control
diazonium_salt_to_aryl_halide_cuxsandmey.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: Sandmeyer CuX vs. KI/heat vs. Balz-Schiemann vs. Gattermann)

genuine_levers: pH control: alkaline medium for coupling with phenol (para position), neutral/slightly acidic for coupling with aniline (para position)
Temperature: must be 0–5°C to prevent decomposition of the arenediazonium salt; aliphatic diazonium salts are unstable and cannot be used
Coupling partner choice: ArOH (phenol) vs ArNH₂ (amine) determines the exact azo dye product
common_traps: Assuming aliphatic diazonium salts can be used for this coupling (they decompose immediately)
Forgetting that pH is critical: too acidic protonates the amine coupling partner, preventing reaction
Mistaking this for a simple electrophilic aromatic substitution without noting the specific para regioselectivity for activated rings
diazonium_salt_to_azo_compound_arohorarn.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (pH and temperature control for diazonium stability and coupling regiochemistry)

genuine_levers: Alkaline pH (NaOH/Na₂CO₃, pH 9–10) is required to form phenoxide nucleophile; acidic pH protonates β-naphthol → coupling fails or is slow
Temperature (0–5°C) is critical to stabilize arenediazonium salt; higher temperature leads to decomposition
Coupling occurs at C-1 (para to OH) of β-naphthol due to high activation; α-naphthol or less activated phenols couple at different positions
common_traps: Confusing aromatic diazonium salt (stable at 0–5°C) with aliphatic diazonium salt (unstable, decomposes instantly) — aliphatic salts cannot be used for azo coupling
Writing the coupling mechanism as SN2 on the diazonium nitrogen; correct mechanism is electrophilic aromatic substitution (ArN₂⁺ as electrophile) on the activated naphthol ring
Forgetting that the reaction requires an activated aromatic coupling partner (phenol, aniline, naphthol); simple benzene does not undergo azo coupling under these conditions
diazonium_salt_to_naphthalene_alkalineco.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (pH and temperature control; distinction between aromatic and aliphatic diazonium stability)

genuine_levers: Sandmeyer cyanation uses CuCN catalyst; direct nucleophilic substitution with NaCN alone fails
Gattermann modification uses Cu powder + HCN as an alternative to CuCN
common_traps: Mistaking this for a standard SN2 substitution; the mechanism involves Cu(I) transferring a CN radical
Forgetting that the diazonium group must be prepared from an aniline via diazotization (NaNO2/HCl, 0-5°C) first
diazonium_salt_to_nitrile_nacn.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (Sandmeyer vs. Gattermann reagent choice; mechanism is radical-based, not ionic)

genuine_levers: Temperature control (0-5°C for diazonium salt formation vs. warm/boil for hydrolysis) — critical to avoid explosive decomposition
Aromatic vs. aliphatic substrate — aliphatic diazonium salts decompose instantly to carbocations; aromatic diazonium salts are stable enough for this hydrolysis step
common_traps: Assuming this is an SN2 reaction — the mechanism is ArSN1 (phenyl cation or tight ion pair), not backside attack
Forgetting that the diazonium group (-N₂⁺) is the best leaving group, converting a poor -NH₂ leaving group into a highly reactive electrophile
Confusing this with Sandmeyer reactions — using H₂O/heat gives phenol; using CuCN, CuCl, etc. gives different products
diazonium_salt_to_phenol_ho.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: ArSN1 vs. SN2; understanding diazonium salt stability and reactivity)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice determines product: H3PO2/H2O gives deaminated ArH (substituted_benzene); CuCl gives Ar-Cl (Sandmeyer); CuBr gives Ar-Br (Sandmeyer); CuCN gives Ar-CN (Sandmeyer); KI gives Ar-I; HBF4/heat gives Ar-F (Balz-Schiemann); H3O+ gives Ar-OH (phenol); ArN2+ + Ar'H (activated) gives azo compound (coupling).
common_traps: Assuming aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N2+) are stable like aromatic ones — they decompose instantly to carbocations.
Forgetting temperature control (0-5°C) for diazonium salt formation/stability leads to decomposition.
Mistaking the mechanism for SN2 — aromatic diazonium substitution proceeds via aryl cation (Ar+) or radical (with H3PO2), not backside attack.
Using aq. conditions for Sandmeyer (CuX) instead of the required anhydrous/cuprous salt conditions.
diazonium_salt_to_substituted_benzene_hp.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity for different products from diazonium salt); Structural (recognizing diazonium as a synthetic handle to install/remove functional groups)

genuine_levers: Low temperature (-40°C) is a genuine lever — it gives the kinetic 1,2-addition product; at higher temperature (40°C), the thermodynamic 1,4-addition product becomes major
1 equivalent of HBr is a genuine lever — excess HBr can lead to a second addition across the remaining alkene
diene_conjugated_to_alkyl_halide_hbr1eq.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the product 3-bromo-1-butene (CH3-CHBr-CH=CH2) is chiral only if the starting diene is unsymmetrical, creating a stereocenter at C-2 upon Br addition; for symmetrical dienes like buta-1,3-diene, the product is achiral
common_traps: Students often write the 1,4-addition product (1-bromo-2-butene) as the major product, forgetting the kinetic control at low temperature
Students may incorrectly draw a simple Markovnikov addition to one alkene, ignoring the conjugated system and allylic cation resonance
Students might assume the product is always chiral, forgetting that symmetrical dienes yield achiral products
diene_conjugated_to_alkyl_halide_hbr1eq.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (kinetic vs thermodynamic control); Structural (regiochemistry of addition to conjugated system)

genuine_levers: Cu/300°C is a catalytic oxidative dehydrogenation (industrial) — different from oxidative cleavage with HIO4 or KMnO4
HIO4 cleaves 1,2-diols to carbonyls (2 HCHO for ethane-1,2-diol), NOT to the same dialdehyde as Cu/300°C
KMnO4 (mild, controlled) can partially oxidize a diol to aldehyde, but over-oxidation to carboxylic acid is a competing risk
common_traps: Confusing Cu/300°C oxidative dehydrogenation with HIO4 oxidative cleavage — they give different products
Assuming any diol oxidation with KMnO4 stops cleanly at the aldehyde stage without careful control
Forgetting that vicinal diol cleavage (HIO4) of HOCH2CH2OH gives two formaldehyde molecules, not glyoxal
diol_to_aldehyde_cu.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: oxidative dehydrogenation vs. oxidative cleavage vs. partial oxidation)

genuine_levers: Excess HI with heat drives exhaustive reduction to alkane; limited HI would stop at alkyl iodide (SN2 product)
Red P + I2 generates PI3 in situ, which is the active electrophile for OH substitution; other reagents (e.g., HBr) give different mechanisms/products
common_traps: Mistaking this for a simple dehydration (diol → alkene) — this is a reductive deoxygenation, not an elimination
Assuming the intermediate triiodide undergoes elimination (E2) instead of reductive cleavage by excess HI
diol_to_alkane_redp.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent-driven mechanism shift: PI3 for SN2 substitution followed by HI for reductive cleavage)

genuine_levers: PI3 substitutes OH with I via SN2 on primary OH; PCl5 or SOCl2 would give Cl instead — halide choice is a genuine lever
Excess PI3 leads to complete substitution of all OH groups; stoichiometric PI3 could lead to partial substitution
Primary OH reacts faster than secondary OH — regioselectivity is a genuine lever if diol is unsymmetrical
common_traps: Assuming PI3 gives the same product as PCl5 or SOCl2 (wrong halide)
Forgetting that excess PI3 on glycerol leads to triiodopropane, which can undergo elimination to allyl iodide or propene — not stopping at the alkyl halide
Applying this to tertiary alcohols (tertiary OH does not undergo SN2 with PI3)
diol_to_alkyl_halide_pi.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: PI3 vs other P-halides; stoichiometry and subsequent elimination)

genuine_levers: Oxidant choice: Hot acidic KMnO4 overoxidises primary OH groups to carboxylic acids (HCOOH), while HIO4 stops at aldehydes/ketones — a genuine lever
Temperature/conditions: Hot acidic KMnO4 cleaves vicinal diol to acids; cold dilute KMnO4 gives syn-dihydroxylation without cleavage
common_traps: Confusing HIO4 (milder, stops at aldehydes/ketones) with KMnO4 (overoxidises to acids) — a classic JEE trap
Forgetting that hot acidic KMnO4 can further oxidise formic acid (HCOOH) to CO2 under extreme conditions
Mistaking the stereochemistry of the starting diol (syn vs anti) as relevant for the cleavage product — stereochemistry is erased in this oxidative cleavage step
diol_to_carboxylic_acid_kmno.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant selectivity: KMnO4 vs HIO4; condition selectivity: hot acidic vs cold dilute)

genuine_levers: Regioselectivity: 1° OH groups (C-1, C-3) are preferentially esterified over the 2° OH (C-2) under mild conditions
Reagent choice: Ac₂O (anhydride) vs CH₃COOH (acid) changes the mechanism and reaction rate, potentially affecting selectivity
common_traps: Assuming all three OH groups react equally — the product is a partially esterified diol, not a fully acetylated triacetate
Confusing this mild, selective acylation with exhaustive esterification under forcing conditions (e.g., excess Ac₂O, strong acid catalyst)
diol_to_diol_2eqchcooh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regioselectivity based on steric hindrance and reagent mechanism)

genuine_levers: Temperature is a critical lever: >5°C leads to explosive decomposition, not controlled esterification
H₂SO₄ acts as a dehydrating agent to generate NO₂⁺; changing the acid (e.g., to HCl) would not yield the nitronium ion
Substrate is a 1,2,3-triol (glycerol); a diol with fewer OH groups yields a partially nitrated ester
diol_to_ester_conchno.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the central carbon of glycerol becomes a stereocenter only if the three nitrate groups are considered different; in practice, the molecule is achiral (C2 symmetry axis)
common_traps: Confusing this with aromatic nitration (electrophilic substitution on benzene) — this is esterification of alcohol OH groups
Forgetting the critical temperature control and writing the reaction at room temperature
Writing the product as a nitro compound (R-NO₂) instead of a nitrate ester (R-O-NO₂)
diol_to_ester_conchno.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent role: H₂SO₄ as dehydrant, not catalyst; temperature as safety/product lever)

genuine_levers: Regiochemistry is a genuine lever: basic conditions (RNH₂ alone) → attack at less hindered epoxide carbon (SN2); acidic conditions (RNH₂ with H⁺) → attack at more substituted carbon (SN1-like)
Stereochemistry is a genuine lever: anti addition of amine to epoxide ring; overall stereochemistry depends on the stereochemistry of the starting epoxide
epoxide_to_alcohol_rnh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the epoxide carbon being attacked is already a stereocenter; the new alcohol and amine groups create a new stereocenter at the attacked carbon, resulting in a trans-β-amino alcohol relative to the existing substituents
common_traps: Assuming the amine attacks the more substituted carbon under all conditions — this is only true under acidic catalysis
Forgetting the stereochemical outcome: the ring-opening is an anti addition, which is critical for determining the final stereochemistry of the β-amino alcohol
Confusing this with the reaction of a tertiary amine, which does not open epoxides via nucleophilic attack but can form amine oxides with oxidants
epoxide_to_alcohol_rnh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry switch based on acid/base conditions); Interpretive (stereochemistry tracking from epoxide formation to ring-opening)

genuine_levers: Acid-catalyzed opening: protonation leads to SN1-like attack at the more substituted carbon (Markovnikov regiochemistry)
Base-catalyzed opening: HO⁻ attacks the less substituted carbon via SN2 (anti-Markovnikov regiochemistry)
Stereochemistry: anti-addition of OH groups is mandatory for both acid and base pathways — compare with syn-addition routes (e.g., OsO4)
epoxide_to_diol_ho.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the epoxide carbons are already chiral or become chiral upon opening; the anti-addition creates two new stereocenters with defined relative stereochemistry (racemic or meso)
common_traps: Assuming syn-addition (common confusion with dihydroxylation via OsO4)
Forgetting that acid and base give opposite regiochemistry but the same anti stereochemistry
Mistaking the epoxide opening for a carbocation rearrangement step (rearrangements are rare unless a tertiary carbocation can form)
epoxide_to_diol_ho.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry switch: acid vs base); Interpretive (stereochemistry: anti-addition is fixed)

genuine_levers: Acidic opening (H3O+) leads to anti-addition of water via SN2-like attack on the MORE substituted carbon (Markovnikov-like regioselectivity)
Basic opening (OH-) leads to anti-addition of hydroxide via SN2-like attack on the LESS substituted carbon (anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity)
18O isotope labeling in the acid-catalyzed mechanism: the labeled oxygen (18O) of the epoxide ends up in the OH group on the carbon NOT attacked by water
epoxide_to_epoxide_h3odiluteaci.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the epoxide is unsymmetrical and the ring-opening creates a carbon with four different substituents; symmetrical epoxides (like cyclohexene oxide) yield achiral diols
common_traps: Confusing acidic (H3O+) and basic (OH-) opening regioselectivity rules
Forgetting that the overall stereochemistry is anti-addition (trans-diol) from a single enantiomer of an epoxide
Incorrectly applying the 18O isotope result: in acidic opening, the 18O from the epoxide is NOT lost; it becomes an 18OH group
epoxide_to_epoxide_h3odiluteaci.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regioselectivity lever: acid vs base); Structural (stereochemistry of anti-addition)

genuine_levers: Acidic conditions (HX) open epoxide at more substituted carbon (SN1-like via oxocarbenium); basic/nucleophilic conditions open at less substituted carbon (SN2-like)
Stereochemistry: anti-addition of X and OH; trans diaxial opening for cyclohexene epoxides
epoxide_to_halohydrin_hx.creates_stereocenters: conditional — creates stereocenters if the epoxide is chiral or if the halohydrin product has a chiral center; anti-addition dictates relative stereochemistry
common_traps: Assuming syn addition overall — the correct sequence is syn epoxidation (mCPBA) followed by anti ring-opening (HX)
Forgetting regioselectivity: under acidic conditions, the halide adds to the more substituted carbon, opposite to basic ring-opening
epoxide_to_halohydrin_hx.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry under acidic vs basic conditions; stereochemical outcome of anti-addition)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: HX opens via protonation and SN1-like attack at more substituted carbon; X2 opens via halonium ion formation, leading to anti addition
Regiochemistry: Acidic (HX) opening follows Markovnikov-like rule; basic or nucleophilic opening attacks less substituted carbon
Stereochemistry: Cyclic epoxide opening gives trans (anti) vicinal dihalide; stereochemistry is inverted at the attacked carbon
epoxide_to_vicinal_dihalide_hx.creates_stereocenters: conditional — creates stereocenters at both carbons if the epoxide is unsymmetrical or chiral; product stereochemistry is defined by anti addition
common_traps: Assuming syn addition for HX opening (it is anti addition overall)
Forgetting that epoxide opening with HX is regioselective (more substituted carbon attacked by X-)
Confusing this with diol formation (using H2O) or amino alcohol formation (using amine)
epoxide_to_vicinal_dihalide_hx.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry and stereochemistry control); Structural (reagent discrimination: HX vs X2 vs other nucleophiles)

genuine_levers: PCl5 cleaves both O-C bonds of the ester: acyl-O bond gives acid chloride, alkyl-O bond gives alkyl chloride — reagent choice is a genuine lever (e.g., SOCl2 typically converts acids to acid chlorides, not esters)
common_traps: Assuming the ester's alkoxy group (R'O) is lost as an alcohol (R'OH) — it is actually lost as an alkyl chloride (R'Cl)
Confusing this with acid chloride formation from a carboxylic acid (which uses SOCl2, PCl3, or PCl5 but yields HCl/SO2 as byproducts, not R'Cl)
ester_to_acid_chloride_pcl5.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent-specific bond cleavage: PCl5 vs other chlorinating agents)

genuine_levers: Temperature control: -78°C stops at aldehyde; warming to room temperature leads to over-reduction to alcohol
Reagent stoichiometry: 1 equivalent DIBALH at -78°C gives aldehyde; 2 equivalents or excess at RT gives alcohol
Solvent choice: dry, aprotic solvent (toluene/hexane) is critical; protic solvents can quench the reagent or alter the outcome
common_traps: Assuming DIBALH always gives aldehyde regardless of temperature (it does not; temperature is a critical lever)
Confusing DIBALH with LiAlH4 (LiAlH4 reduces ester directly to primary alcohol, not aldehyde)
Forgetting the aqueous workup step is required to liberate the aldehyde from the aluminium complex
ester_to_aldehyde_dibalhdiisob.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and condition selectivity: DIBALH vs LiAlH4; temperature control as a critical lever)

genuine_levers: Acid hydrolysis is reversible; base hydrolysis (saponification) is irreversible and yields carboxylate salt
Excess water drives equilibrium forward; excess alcohol drives reverse (esterification)
common_traps: Confusing acid hydrolysis (reversible, AAc2) with base hydrolysis (irreversible, BAc2)
Forgetting to acidify the product after base hydrolysis to get the free carboxylic acid
Writing SN2 instead of nucleophilic acyl substitution (AAC2) mechanism
ester_to_carboxylic_acid_h3odiluteaci.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: AAc2 vs BAc2; reversibility: acid vs base hydrolysis)

genuine_levers: aq NaOH → BAc2 mechanism, irreversible hydrolysis to carboxylate; alc. NaOH → alkoxide, can lead to transesterification (different product)
¹⁸O labeling: OH⁻ attacks acyl carbon, so ¹⁸O from NaOH ends up in carboxylate product (acyl-O cleavage) — a key mechanistic tracer
common_traps: Confusing BAc2 (base-catalyzed, irreversible) with AAc2 (acid-catalyzed, reversible) — acid hydrolysis uses H3O+ and is reversible
Writing 'ester + NaOH → alcohol + aldehyde' — NaOH hydrolyzes to carboxylate, not aldehyde
Forgetting that the product under basic conditions is the carboxylate salt (RCOO⁻Na⁺), not the free acid (RCOOH) until acidified
ester_to_ester_aqnaoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: BAc2 vs AAc2; ¹⁸O tracer analysis)

genuine_levers: Mechanism switch (AAc1 vs AAc2): conc. H₂SO₄/MeOH on a pivalate ester (R = t-Bu) gives AAc1 → ether; normal esters (e.g., MeOAc) under same conditions give AAc2 → transesterification (different ester).
Substrate sterics: Only highly hindered esters (e.g., pivalate) undergo AAc1; less hindered esters follow AAc2.
CO evolution is a diagnostic clue for the AAc1 mechanism.
common_traps: Applying AAc1 mechanism to all esters — students often miss the steric requirement and predict ether for any ester with conc. H₂SO₄/MeOH.
Predicting transesterification (the AAc2 product) for pivalate esters, missing the unique AAc1 pathway.
Confusing this with Fischer esterification (carboxylic acid + alcohol → ester) or its reverse.
ester_to_ether_conchso.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: AAc1 vs AAc2 based on substrate sterics)

genuine_levers: Temperature control: Low T (<100°C) → ortho product (kinetic/thermodynamic intramolecular delivery). High T (>150°C) → para product (thermodynamic control).
Photo-Fries (UV light, RT) → predominantly ortho product.
Substrate limitation: Only phenyl esters (phenol-derived esters) undergo this rearrangement.
common_traps: Assuming the product is a simple ester hydrolysis product (phenol + carboxylic acid) instead of a ketone.
Forgetting that AlCl₃ is required in >1 equivalent (catalytic amount insufficient) and that anhydrous conditions are critical.
Applying standard Friedel-Crafts acylation rules directly (e.g., expecting meta-substitution if ring is deactivated).
ester_to_ketone_alcl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature-dependent regioselectivity; distinguishing Fries rearrangement from simple hydrolysis or intermolecular FC acylation)

genuine_levers: Temperature controls regiochemistry: high temperature (>150°C) favors para product; low temperature favors ortho product
Substrate requirement: must be an aryl ester (Ar-O-CO-R); alkyl esters do not undergo Fries rearrangement
common_traps: Confusing Fries with Friedel-Crafts acylation — Fries uses an aryl ester as the acyl source, not an acyl halide
Assuming the acyl group migrates to the same carbon as the OH group — it migrates to an adjacent ring carbon (ortho or para)
Forgetting that AlCl₃ cleaves the ester O-CO bond, not the Ar-O bond
ester_to_substituted_benzene_alcl3.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (temperature-dependent regioselectivity; mechanism involving O-to-C acyl migration)

genuine_levers: HI vs HBr vs HCl reactivity: HI cleaves fastest (I⁻ best nucleophile) and can cleave aryl ethers to PhI with excess
Mechanism switch: SN1 for 3°/2° alkyl ethers (HI/HBr, acidic) vs SN2 for 1° alkyl ethers
Aryl ethers (PhOR) cleave at alkyl-oxygen bond → PhOH + R-X (aryl C-O bond too strong for SN on ring)
ether_to_alkyl_halide_conchxhihbra.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alkyl halide product carbon is chiral and the mechanism (SN1 vs SN2) affects stereochemistry; SN1 leads to racemization, SN2 leads to inversion
common_traps: Assuming HCl cleaves ethers as readily as HI/HBr (HCl is much slower/ineffective)
Forgetting that the alcohol initially formed can be further halogenated to a second alkyl halide with excess HX
Applying SN1 mechanism to 1° alkyl ethers (should be SN2)
Assuming aryl ethers (PhOR) cleave to give Ph-X (instead of PhOH + R-X)
ether_to_alkyl_halide_conchxhihbra.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SN1 vs SN2 based on substrate; reagent selectivity: HI > HBr >> HCl)

genuine_levers: BF₃ is essential Lewis acid catalyst — without it, no reaction; other Lewis acids may give different reactivity or no reaction
CO insertion is the key step — changing to other electrophiles (e.g., alkyl halides) would give different products (e.g., alkylated ethers)
ether_to_ester_co.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the ether oxygen is attached to a chiral carbon; the CO insertion step does not inherently create new stereocenters, but if the oxocarbenium intermediate is chiral and the reaction proceeds with retention, chirality may be preserved
common_traps: Confusing with ester hydrolysis (ester → ether) — this is the reverse transformation
Assuming a simple substitution — this is a carbonylation (CO insertion), not a typical nucleophilic substitution
Mistaking BF₃ for a Brønsted acid — it's a Lewis acid that activates the ether, not a proton source
ether_to_ester_co.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: Lewis acid activation followed by CO insertion, not a common reaction)

genuine_levers: Radical initiator (e.g., light, trace peroxide) is required for the chain process; without it, the reaction does not proceed at a significant rate.
Alpha-C selectivity: the radical abstracts the hydrogen from the carbon alpha to the oxygen, not from other positions.
The presence of oxygen (O₂) is essential; in its absence, the radical would dimerize or disproportionate instead of forming a hydroperoxide.
common_traps: Assuming this is a standard ionic substitution or addition reaction; it is a radical chain process.
Forgetting that the product is a hydroperoxide (ether-OOH), not a simple oxidized ether like an aldehyde or ketone.
Overlooking the explosive hazard of accumulated ether peroxides upon concentration or heating.
ether_to_ether_oairslowradi.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the radical chain mechanism and its specific selectivity)

genuine_levers: Thermal conditions (Δ) are essential for the [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement; acid or base catalysts would lead to different reactions (e.g., acid-catalyzed cleavage of ethers)
If the ortho position on the aromatic ring is blocked, the reaction proceeds via a second Cope rearrangement to give the para-allylphenol (Claisen-Cope cascade)
ether_to_phenol_thermalnocat.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a stereocenter is created at the γ-carbon of the migrating allyl group; the configuration (E/Z) of the starting allyl ether determines the stereochemistry of the new allylic carbon in the product (stereospecific with inversion)
common_traps: Assuming this is a simple substitution or acid-catalyzed reaction — the thermal, pericyclic mechanism is non-ionic and distinct
Forgetting that the product carbon skeleton changes (the allyl group migrates from O to C, altering connectivity) and drawing the original ether structure incorrectly
ether_to_phenol_thermalnocat.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism recognition: pericyclic vs ionic); Structural (regiochemistry: ortho vs para migration based on substitution)

genuine_levers: HCHO (formaldehyde) gives a primary alcohol with one extra carbon; any other aldehyde (RCHO) gives a secondary alcohol; ketone (R2CO) gives a tertiary alcohol
Anhydrous conditions are mandatory — moisture destroys Grignard reagent before reaction
Ester + RMgX gives tertiary alcohol (two additions); amide + RMgX gives ketone (one addition after workup)
grignard_to_alcohol_hcho.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carbonyl carbon of the aldehyde/ketone (other than HCHO) is prochiral and the Grignard R group is different from the existing substituents
common_traps: Forgetting that HCHO is the only carbonyl giving a primary alcohol
Assuming ester + RMgX stops at ketone (it proceeds to tertiary alcohol)
Using aqueous conditions destroys Grignard (RMgX + H2O → RH)
grignard_to_alcohol_hcho.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (carbonyl substrate selectivity: HCHO vs RCHO vs ketone vs ester vs amide); Structural (product carbon count and alcohol class)

genuine_levers: CO₂ is a unique electrophile for Grignard reagents, giving a carboxylic acid after workup; other carbonyls (aldehydes, ketones, esters) give alcohols
The reaction adds ONE carbon; product RCOOH has one more carbon than the alkyl group R in RMgX
common_traps: Confusing this with ester + Grignard (which gives tertiary alcohol via double addition); CO₂ gives acid, ester gives alcohol
Forgetting the aqueous workup (H₃O⁺) step to protonate the carboxylate salt to the free acid
Thinking the reaction requires two equivalents of Grignard (only one is needed; carboxylate is not electrophilic)
grignard_to_carboxylic_acid_co.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing CO₂ as a distinct, one-carbon electrophile for Grignard addition)

genuine_levers: NaBH₄ is selective for aldehydes/ketones; LiAlH₄ reduces a broader range (esters, acids, amides) — reagent choice is a genuine lever
Protic solvent (EtOH) vs anhydrous ether for LiAlH₄ — condition choice dictates reagent stability and mechanism
ketone_to_alcohol_nabh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter is created at the carbonyl carbon, resulting in a racemic mixture unless the substrate is prochiral and the reducing agent is chiral or the substrate has a pre-existing chiral center inducing diastereoselectivity
common_traps: NaBH₄ does NOT reduce esters, carboxylic acids, or amides — a common overreach
Assuming LiAlH₄ can be used in protic solvents (it reacts violently with H₂O/ROH)
Forgetting that the product is a racemic secondary alcohol unless stereoselective conditions are specified
ketone_to_alcohol_nabh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: NaBH₄ vs LiAlH₄); Structural (stereochemistry if prochiral ketone specified)

genuine_levers: SeO2 oxidises the alpha-methylene or alpha-methyl group of a ketone to a carbonyl, producing an α-diketone or α-ketoaldehyde
Minimum of two alpha-hydrogens required on the carbon being oxidised; -CH3 > -CH2- in selectivity
common_traps: SeO2 does NOT reduce a ketone to an aldehyde; it oxidises the alpha-carbon, so the original carbonyl remains
SeO2 oxidation is NOT a general method for converting any ketone to an aldehyde; it is specific for alpha-carbon oxidation
SeO2 is also used for allylic oxidation of alkenes, which is a different transformation
ketone_to_aldehyde_seo2selenium.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent specificity: SeO2 for alpha-oxidation vs other reagents for carbonyl reduction or oxidation)

genuine_levers: Wolff-Kishner (NH₂NH₂/KOH) reduces C=O to CH₂; Clemmensen (Zn-Hg/HCl) is the alternative for acid-sensitive substrates
Wolff-Kishner reduces both C=O and C=C (exception case) — standard carbonyl reductions (NaBH₄, LiAlH₄) do NOT reduce isolated C=C
common_traps: Confusing Wolff-Kishner with Clemmensen — both give alkane from ketone, but conditions differ (basic vs acidic)
Forgetting the high-temperature step (KOH/ethylene glycol, reflux) — hydrazone formation alone does not give the product
Applying Wolff-Kishner to substrates with other reducible groups (e.g., nitro, nitrile) without considering side reactions
ketone_to_alkane_nhnh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: Wolff-Kishner vs Clemmensen vs standard carbonyl reductions); Exception handling (C=C reduction)

genuine_levers: Only methyl ketones (CH3COR) react; other ketones (e.g., RCH2COR') are negative
Secondary alcohols with -CHOH-CH3 group give positive test (oxidized in situ to methyl ketone)
Acid derivatives (esters, amides, etc.) with CH3CO- group are negative despite having the group
common_traps: Assuming all carbonyls with an alpha-CH3 give iodoform (esters, acids, amides do NOT)
Forgetting that cyclohexanone gives a positive test via ring cleavage to sodium adipate
Mistaking EtOAc as directly positive (it's positive only after saponification to ethanol)
ketone_to_alkyl_halide_i2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate scope: methyl ketone vs. other carbonyls; in situ oxidation of alcohols)

genuine_levers: Ketone must have at least one α-H to enolise — no reaction if α-carbon is quaternary
Primary amine (RNH2) gives secondary Mannich base (N-H); secondary amine (R2NH) gives tertiary Mannich base (no N-H) — amine nucleophile identity is a lever
Formaldehyde (HCHO) is the typical electrophile; other aldehydes give substituted β-aminocarbonyls (crossed Mannich)
ketone_to_amine_hheat.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter forms at the β-carbon (the carbon bearing the amine) if the ketone enolate attacks the iminium from one face; product is often racemic under standard conditions
common_traps: Using a ketone without α-H (e.g., di‑tert‑butyl ketone) gives no reaction — a common trap
Confusing with reductive amination (ketone + amine + reducing agent → amine) — Mannich uses HCHO and an enol
Forgetting the three‑component nature (ketone + HCHO + amine) and writing a two‑component condensation
ketone_to_amine_hheat.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (three‑component coupling; enolate vs imine chemistry); Structural (new C–C bond formation at α‑position)

genuine_levers: Ylide carbon substituents determine alkene substituents — change from H2C=PPh3 to RCH=PPh3 gives substituted alkene (RHC=CHR')
Ketone vs aldehyde: both work, but aldehyde gives terminal alkene (RHC=CH2) unless ylide is substituted
ketone_to_carbene_methylenepho.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the ylide is non-symmetrical (R1R2C=PPh3) AND the ketone is prochiral, creating a new chiral alkene (E/Z isomerism, not a stereocenter); Wittig does NOT create new tetrahedral stereocenters
common_traps: Wittig gives alkene, NOT alcohol — do not confuse with Grignard addition (RMgX → alcohol)
Ylide carbon is nucleophilic, NOT electrophilic — it attacks the carbonyl carbon
Product is exocyclic alkene (C=CH2) for methylenetriphenylphosphorane, NOT endocyclic unless ylide is part of a ring
ketone_to_carbene_methylenepho.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (ylide structure determines alkene substitution pattern); Structural (C=O to C=C transformation)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: HCN (acidic) vs. KCN (basic) dictates mechanism — HCN gives acid-catalyzed addition (slower), KCN gives direct nucleophilic attack by CN⁻
Substrate reactivity: Aldehydes react faster than ketones; sterically hindered ketones give poor yields due to unfavorable equilibrium
ketone_to_cyanohydrin_hcn.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter is created at the carbonyl carbon, but the product is racemic unless a chiral catalyst or enzyme is used
common_traps: Forgetting that the product is an α-hydroxy nitrile (cyanohydrin), not a nitrile or an imine
Assuming the reaction is irreversible — equilibrium lies to the left for ketones, requiring careful conditions
Confusing with other carbonyl additions (e.g., Grignard gives tertiary alcohol, not cyanohydrin)
ketone_to_cyanohydrin_hcn.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism and equilibrium control); Structural (recognizing cyanohydrin functional group)

genuine_levers: Migratory aptitude order (H > 3° > 2° > Ph > 1° > CH3) determines which group migrates to form the ester — a classic JEE selectivity lever
Cyclic ketones undergo ring expansion to lactones — ring size changes
Configuration of the migrating chiral center is retained (suprafacial migration)
ketone_to_ester_mcpbametachl.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the migrating group is chiral; stereochemistry is retained, not created de novo
common_traps: Assuming migratory aptitude follows carbocation stability (it does not; H migrates fastest)
Forgetting that cyclopropyl migrates slower than primary alkyl (opposite to SN1 reactivity)
Applying the reaction to tertiary amines (they form amine oxides with peracids, not esters)
ketone_to_ester_mcpbametachl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (migratory aptitude is counterintuitive and a major JEE trap); Structural (regiochemistry for unsymmetrical ketones)

genuine_levers: Acid-catalyzed acetal formation (ketone + ROH + H⁺) yields ether (acetal); base-catalyzed aldol or enolate alkylation yields a different product — catalyst choice is a genuine lever
Using a diol (e.g., ethylene glycol) yields a cyclic acetal; using a mono-alcohol yields an acyclic acetal — alcohol structure is a genuine lever
ketone_to_ether_roh.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter is created at the acetal carbon (C(OR)₂) only if the two alkoxy groups are different; with identical R groups or a symmetric diol, the product is achiral
common_traps: Mistaking this for Fischer esterification (carboxylic acid + ROH + H⁺) which yields ester, not acetal
Forgetting that acetals are protecting groups and are hydrolyzed back to ketone with aqueous acid — writing them as irreversible final products is a trap
ketone_to_ether_roh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (catalyst role: acid for acetal formation vs base for enolate chemistry); Structural (if a chiral acetal is formed)

genuine_levers: Acidic conditions (NaOAc buffer) stop at mono-halogenation; basic conditions (NaOH) lead to polyhalogenation and haloform reaction
Reagent choice: X2 (Cl2, Br2, I2) determines the halogen introduced; NaOAc provides acidic buffer to prevent base catalysis
ketone_to_ketone_x2.creates_stereocenters: conditional — if the α-carbon of the starting ketone is prochiral, halogenation creates a stereocenter; for symmetrical ketones (e.g., acetone), no stereocenter is created
common_traps: Mistaking this for base-catalysed halogenation (which gives trihalomethyl ketone and haloform)
Assuming the reaction requires a strong acid (it uses a weak acid buffer)
Forgetting that the α-carbon must have at least one α-H for enolisation to occur
ketone_to_ketone_x2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (acid vs base catalysis distinction for α-halogenation); Structural if the product is chiral

genuine_levers: Cyclic ketone substrate leads to ring expansion by one CH2, forming a lactone; acyclic ketone gives chain-elongated ketone
Migration aptitude (H > Ph > R) determines which group migrates, affecting product structure
ketone_to_lactone_diazomethane.creates_stereocenters: conditional — stereochemistry of the migrating group is inverted if it is chiral, due to anti-periplanar migration requirement
common_traps: Confusing this with Baeyer-Villiger oxidation (peracid oxidizes ketone to ester/lactone) — reagents and mechanism are different
Assuming the most substituted alkyl group migrates fastest (correct for carbocation rearrangements, but here H migrates fastest)
Thinking aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N2+) are stable intermediates (they are extremely unstable and lose N2 instantly)
ketone_to_lactone_diazomethane.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (migration aptitude order is counterintuitive and contrasts with Baeyer-Villiger); Structural (ring expansion vs chain elongation)

genuine_levers: pH control (buffered acidic) is critical: too acidic protonates NH2OH, too basic deprotonates it, both slow reaction
Reagent choice: NH2OH·HCl + base (pyridine/NaOAc) gives oxime; NH2NH2 (hydrazine) gives hydrazone; 2,4-DNP gives 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone — reagent identity is a genuine lever
Product geometry: oximes exist as E/Z isomers (syn/anti) about C=N bond; formation can be stereoselective
common_traps: Confusing oxime formation with Beckmann rearrangement — Beckmann is a separate reaction requiring strong acid/catalyst (H2SO4, PCl5)
Writing NH2OH alone without the acid/base pair — the actual reagent is hydroxylamine hydrochloride plus a mild base to generate free NH2OH in situ
Forgetting that oximes from unsymmetrical ketones can give two isomeric oximes (though not stereocenters, they are constitutional isomers)
ketone_to_oxime_nhohhcl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: oxime vs other C=N derivatives); Structural (E/Z isomerism of product)

genuine_levers: Migration aptitude order: -COOEt (ester) > Ph (aryl) > Me (alkyl) — the better migrating group determines product skeleton
Driving force: aromatisation — the reaction is irreversible and proceeds due to formation of a stable aromatic ring
common_traps: Assuming the product phenol has the same carbon skeleton as the starting ketone — the 1,2-shift changes connectivity
Applying the wrong migration aptitude order (e.g., Ar > R) — in this specific rearrangement, an ester group migrates faster than an aryl group
ketone_to_phenol_h.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (skeletal rearrangement); Interpretive (migration aptitude order is counter-intuitive and must be memorised for this specific reaction)

genuine_levers: LAH reduces lactam to cyclic amine; milder reductants (e.g., NaBH4) do NOT reduce amides/lactams — reagent choice is a genuine lever
lactam_to_amine_lah.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the lactam ring carbon being reduced was prochiral (i.e., attached to two different groups besides C=O and N); product stereochemistry is racemic
common_traps: NaBH4 is often incorrectly used for amide/lactam reduction; it is ineffective
Product is a cyclic amine, NOT an amino alcohol — the ring C=O is reduced to CH2, not cleaved
lactam_to_amine_lah.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reductant selectivity: LAH vs NaBH4 for carbonyls)

genuine_levers: Nylon-6,6: copolymerization of hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid (6C diamine + 6C diacid) → alternating amide bonds
Nylon-6: homopolymerization of caprolactam (6-membered cyclic lactam) via ring-opening → uniform amide bonds
common_traps: Confusing Nylon-6,6 (two monomers) with Nylon-6 (one monomer) — the '6,6' vs '6' notation is a key lever
Assuming both polymers have identical properties — Nylon-6,6 has higher melting point due to more symmetric H-bonding
lactam_to_lactam_heat.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (monomer identity and polymer type discrimination: copolymer vs homopolymer)

genuine_levers: Catalyst choice (V2O5) and temperature (400-500°C) are essential for selective oxidation to anhydride; milder oxidants (KMnO4) would over-oxidise or give different products
Substrate is naphthalene (two fused benzenes) — benzene under same conditions gives maleic anhydride, not phthalic anhydride
common_traps: Drawing phthalic acid (the acid) as the final product instead of the cyclic anhydride (loss of water)
Forgetting that two carbons are lost as CO2, leading to a C10 product instead of the correct C8 phthalic anhydride
Confusing this with benzene oxidation to maleic anhydride, which is a different product
naphthalene_to_anhydride_o2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (industrial process recall: specific catalyst/temp for naphthalene vs benzene oxidation)

genuine_levers: Temperature control: ≤80°C yields α-sulphonic acid (kinetic product); ≥110°C yields β-sulphonic acid (thermodynamic product) — temperature is a genuine lever
Reversibility of sulphonation at high temperature allows thermodynamic control — this is a classic reversible EAS example
common_traps: Assuming sulphonation is irreversible like nitration or halogenation — it is NOT, reversibility is key for temperature effect
Predicting α-product at all temperatures — forgetting the switch to β-product at high T
Applying simple benzene EAS ortho/para directing rules without considering naphthalene's unique α/β reactivity
naphthalene_to_naphthalene_conch2so4.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (kinetic vs thermodynamic control in EAS; reversibility of sulphonation)

genuine_levers: DIBALH stops at aldehyde (partial reduction); LiAlH4 reduces nitrile fully to primary amine — reductant choice is a genuine lever
DIBALH reduces ester to aldehyde (partial reduction), but over-reduces ester to alcohol — this is an exception case
common_traps: DIBALH does NOT reduce nitrile to primary amine — that is LiAlH4
DIBALH over-reduces ester to alcohol — this is an exception to its typical partial reduction behavior
nitrile_to_aldehyde_dibalhdiisob.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reductant selectivity: DIBALH vs LiAlH4 for nitrile reduction, including the ester exception)

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces nitrile to primary amine (CH2-NH2); Na/EtOH (Bouveault–Blanc) reduces nitrile to aldehyde (partial reduction) — reagent choice is a genuine lever
H2/Ni (catalytic hydrogenation) reduces nitrile to primary amine; Pd/C with H2 may over-reduce or give different selectivity — catalyst choice matters
common_traps: LiAlH4 reduction of nitrile gives primary amine, NOT an imine intermediate that is isolable — the imine is reduced in situ
Na/EtOH reduction of nitrile is NOT a standard method for amine synthesis; it typically gives aldehyde (partial reduction) unless conditions are forcing
Gabriel synthesis uses phthalimide to make primary amines from alkyl halides; it is NOT a method to reduce nitriles — confusing synthesis of amine from halide vs reduction of nitrile
nitrile_to_amine_h.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: LiAlH4 vs Na/EtOH vs H2/Ni for nitrile reduction)

genuine_levers: Reaction conditions determine intermediate: acid hydrolysis (H3O+/Δ) proceeds via amide; basic hydrolysis (NaOH/Δ) proceeds via carboxylate salt
Controlled conditions (e.g., milder acid, limited water) can stop the reaction at the primary amide stage
common_traps: Forgetting the two-step addition of water (nitrile → amide → acid) and writing a direct conversion
In basic hydrolysis, forgetting the acidic workup step to convert the carboxylate salt (R-COO⁻Na⁺) to the free carboxylic acid (R-COOH)
nitrile_to_carboxylic_acid_ho.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism and intermediate control: acid vs base hydrolysis)

genuine_levers: Nitrile + 1 equivalent of Grignard reagent yields ketone after workup; nitrile + 2 equivalents of Grignard yields tertiary alcohol after workup — stoichiometry is a genuine lever
For a dinitrile (e.g., NC-CN), each nitrile group reacts independently with 2 equivalents of Grignard, yielding two ketone molecules
common_traps: Mistaking this for ester + Grignard (which yields tertiary alcohol, not ketone, due to over-addition)
Forgetting the acidic workup (H3O+) step required to hydrolyze the ketimine intermediate to the final ketone
Assuming the reaction stops after the first Grignard addition to the nitrile; the intermediate is an imine salt that is stable until workup
nitrile_to_ketone_12ch3mgbr.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (stoichiometry control to stop at ketone vs. tertiary alcohol; comparison with ester/amide Grignard reactivity)

genuine_levers: Monomer must contain an electron-withdrawing group (EWG) adjacent to C=C (e.g., -CN, -COOR, -CHO) to stabilise the propagating carbanion — without EWG, anionic polymerisation fails
Choice of initiator (R-Li vs KNH₂ vs NaNH₂) affects initiation rate and chain end structure, but product remains a nitrile polymer
Living polymerisation: chain remains active until quenched — addition of a different monomer yields block copolymers, not random/homopolymer
common_traps: Assuming termination steps (like coupling/disproportionation) occur — anionic polymerisation has no spontaneous termination; chain stays 'living'
Using anionic polymerisation on electron-rich alkenes (e.g., ethylene, propene) — these require radical/cationic methods
Confusing anionic with radical polymerisation — no radical initiators (e.g., peroxides, AIBN) are involved here
nitrile_to_nitrile_rlinbutyllit.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (monomer selectivity: EWG requirement for anionic polymerisation; living polymer concept)

genuine_levers: Nitrile + 1 eq. Grignard → ketone (stops at ketone); Ester + 1 eq. Grignard → ketone intermediate → reacts with second eq. → tertiary alcohol (cannot isolate ketone); Amide + 1 eq. Grignard → ketone (isolable) — nucleophile count and leaving group stability are genuine levers
nitrile_to_substituted_benzene_1ch3mgbr.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the newly formed sp3 carbon (from Grignard addition) in the ketone product becomes chiral; requires the nitrile R group and the Grignard R group to be different, and the carbonyl carbon to bear two different substituents after reaction
common_traps: Assuming nitrile + Grignard gives tertiary alcohol (like ester) — it stops at ketone
Assuming ester + Grignard gives ketone — it proceeds to tertiary alcohol
Forgetting that the imine anion intermediate from nitrile is less electrophilic, blocking a second Grignard addition
nitrile_to_substituted_benzene_1ch3mgbr.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (nucleophile count and carbonyl derivative reactivity hierarchy: nitrile vs. ester vs. amide + Grignard)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Zn/NaOH (2 mol) gives hydrazobenzene, then acid-catalyzed rearrangement to benzidine; LiAlH4 in dry ether also gives hydrazobenzene; Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral) gives phenylhydroxylamine, not aniline
common_traps: Assuming reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 directly (ignoring hydrazobenzene intermediate and benzidine rearrangement)
Applying EAS rules from the nitro compound instead of the aniline product (product regiochemistry is determined by the amine group, not the original nitro group)
nitro_compound_to_aniline_znnaoh2molor.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent-specific product differentiation: Zn/NaOH vs Zn/NH4Cl vs LAH)

genuine_levers: 1 equiv Zn/NaOH reduces nitro to nitroso, enabling bimolecular coupling to azo; excess Zn/NaOH would reduce nitro to aniline (different product)
Regiochemistry of EAS on substituted benzene ring(s) determines final product structure — substituent directing effects are a genuine lever
nitro_compound_to_azo_compound_znnaoh1mo.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the azo compound is unsymmetrical and the N=N bond is not in a symmetric environment; the thermodynamic trans product is achiral, but cis-azobenzene is chiral if substituents make the two halves different
common_traps: Assuming excess Zn/NaOH gives azo compound (it gives aniline)
Forgetting that the reaction gives the trans-azo isomer as the thermodynamic product under these conditions
Incorrectly applying EAS rules to predict regiochemistry when coupling two different substituted nitroarenes
nitro_compound_to_azo_compound_znnaoh1mo.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (stoichiometry control: 1 equiv vs excess Zn); Structural (regiochemistry via EAS on substituted rings)

genuine_levers: Sn/HCl is a reducing agent for nitro group to amine; other reagents (e.g., Zn/NH4Cl) reduce nitro to hydroxylamine or azoxy compounds — reagent choice determines final product
Basification (NaOH) after Sn/HCl reduction is required to liberate the free aniline from the amine-SnCl2 complex; omitting basification yields the complex, not the free amine
common_traps: Confusing Sn/HCl (reduction to amine) with Sn/conc HCl + NaNO2 + CuX (Sandmeyer reaction to aryl halide) — both use Sn/HCl but subsequent steps differ
Assuming Sn/HCl reduces other functional groups (like carbonyls) under these conditions — it is selective for nitro group in aromatic systems
nitro_compound_to_substituted_benzene_sn.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: Sn/HCl vs Zn/NH4Cl vs H2/Pd for nitro reduction; and sequence awareness: basification step)

genuine_levers: Acid catalysis (H⁺) or heat (Δ) is required for E/Z interconversion; at RT, the isomers are isolable and distinct
Beckmann rearrangement outcome depends on E/Z geometry: the group anti to the OH migrates
common_traps: Forgetting that the lone pair on nitrogen counts as a 'substituent' for geometric isomerism analysis
Assuming E/Z interconversion is rapid at RT (it is not; barrier is ~20–25 kcal/mol)
Incorrectly predicting the Beckmann rearrangement product by not identifying which group is anti to the OH
oxime_to_oxime_notinterconv.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (geometric isomerism analysis with lone pair as a group; Beckmann rearrangement stereospecificity)

genuine_levers: Reimer-Tiemann uses :CCl₂ electrophile — if both ortho positions are blocked, formylation occurs at para position (e.g., guaiacol → vanillin)
Substrate substitution pattern (ortho vs para) is a genuine lever — one ortho blocked (e.g., OMe) directs :CCl₂ to the free ortho or para; two orthos blocked forces para substitution
common_traps: Assuming CHCl₃ is the direct electrophile (the real electrophile is :CCl₂)
Assuming phenol halogenation requires a Lewis acid catalyst (phenol brominates without catalyst in water)
Assuming phenol reacts with NaHCO₃ (only phenols with pKa < ~6.3, like 2,4-dinitrophenol, do)
phenol_to_aldehyde_naoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate-directed regiochemistry in electrophilic aromatic substitution)

genuine_levers: Industrial gas-phase oxidation with V₂O₅/O₂ at 400–450°C → ring-opened product (maleic anhydride); milder oxidants (e.g., CrO₃) would not cleave the aromatic ring
common_traps: Students may confuse this with simple phenol oxidation to quinones; this is a deep-selective industrial oxidation requiring high temperature and a specific catalyst
Mistaking benzene → maleic anhydride conditions as identical to phenol → maleic anhydride; the substrate reactivity differs
phenol_to_anhydride_ovoindustria.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing that specific industrial conditions lead to ring cleavage vs. typical functional group transformations)

genuine_levers: PCl₅ reacts slowly with phenol vs fast with aliphatic alcohols due to partial C-O double bond character from resonance
Alternative route: Sandmeyer reaction (ArN₂⁺ + HCl/Cu) gives better yield for aryl chloride synthesis
common_traps: Assuming phenol reacts with PCl₅ as readily as aliphatic alcohols to give high yield
Forgetting that phenol halogenation with Br₂/H₂O requires no Lewis acid catalyst (unlike benzene)
Thinking phenol reacts with NaHCO₃ to give CO₂ (it does not; only phenols with pKa < ~6.3 do)
phenol_to_aryl_halide_pclslowreact.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing phenol's unique reactivity: slow with PCl₅, no catalyst needed for electrophilic substitution, acidity nuances with NaHCO₃)

genuine_levers: Zn/Δ gives benzene (reductive deoxygenation); H₂/Pd gives cyclohexanol (ring hydrogenation) — reductant choice and conditions are genuine levers
common_traps: Assuming phenol reacts with NaHCO₃ to give CO₂ — it does not (pKa ~10 > H₂CO₃ pKa ~6.35)
Assuming halogenation of phenol requires a Lewis acid catalyst — it does not; Br₂/H₂O gives tribromination instantly
Assuming all nitrophenols react with NaHCO₃ — only those with pKa < ~6.3 (e.g., 2,4-dinitrophenol) do
phenol_to_benzene_znreductionl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity for reduction vs hydrogenation; acidity comparisons and exceptions)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: RCOCl (acyl chloride) + base (pyridine/Et3N) gives O-acylation (ester) at RT; (RCO)2O (acyl anhydride) can also give ester, especially with base or phenoxide
Condition choice: O-acylation (ester) is favored at RT; C-acylation (ketone via Fries rearrangement) requires AlCl3 + heat
common_traps: Assuming phenol reacts with NaHCO3 (it does NOT; pKa ~10 > H2CO3 pKa ~6.35)
Assuming all nitrophenols react with NaHCO3 (only those with pKa < ~6.3, like 2,4-dinitrophenol, do)
Confusing O-acylation (ester) with C-acylation (ketone) — the latter requires Fries rearrangement conditions (Lewis acid + heat)
Assuming phenol gives a positive CAN test (it does NOT; aliphatic 1°/2° alcohols give red, phenol gives brown/green like 3° alcohols)
phenol_to_ester_rcoclacylchl.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent/condition selectivity: O-acylation vs C-acylation; acidity comparisons with NaHCO3)

genuine_levers: Persulfate oxidation: K2S2O8/KOH (Elbs) gives para-dihydroxybenzene (hydroquinone); (NH4)2S2O8 (Boyland-Sims) gives ortho-dihydroxybenzene (catechol) — oxidant choice is a genuine lever
common_traps: Confusing this aromatic substitution with alkene dihydroxylation (OsO4) — this is an electrophilic aromatic substitution, not an addition to a double bond
Assuming the product is a geminal diol — the product is an aromatic para-diol (hydroquinone), not a 1,1-diol
phenol_to_geminal_dihalide_kso.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant selectivity: Elbs vs. Boyland-Sims para vs ortho control)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice determines product: KMnO₄/Na₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ oxidises phenol to p-benzoquinone; conc HNO₃/H₂SO₄ nitrates the ring (different product) — reagent identity is a genuine lever
Ring substitution pattern matters: 2,4-dinitrophenol (deactivated ring) is harder to oxidise to quinone than phenol itself
common_traps: Assuming any strong oxidant (KMnO₄) oxidises phenol to a carboxylic acid — it stops at quinone
Confusing this oxidation with nitration (conc HNO₃/H₂SO₄) which gives nitro-phenol, not quinone
phenol_to_ketone_kmnoornacroh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: oxidation vs nitration; understanding quinone formation)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Na metal (dry) vs NaOH (aq) vs NaHCO₃ (aq) — determines if reaction occurs and mechanism (acid-base vs redox)
Acidity comparison: Phenol (pKa~10) reacts with NaOH (H₂O pKa 15.7) but NOT with NaHCO₃ (H₂CO₃ pKa 6.35) — a classic JEE lever
common_traps: Assuming phenol reacts with NaHCO₃ (it does not; only carboxylic acids and phenols with pKa < ~6 do)
Assuming NaOH reacts with aliphatic alcohols (it does not; only Na metal does)
Confusing the acidity order of substituted phenols (e.g., p-nitrophenol > phenol but still does NOT react with NaHCO₃)
phenol_to_phenol_naornaoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (acid-base reactivity: comparing pKa values to predict reaction with Na, NaOH, NaHCO₃, Na₂CO₃)

genuine_levers: Acid catalyst (H⁺) yields linear Novolac resin (thermoplastic); Base catalyst (NaOH) yields crosslinked Bakelite (thermoset) — catalyst choice is a genuine lever
Phenol:formaldehyde ratio > 1:1 yields Novolac; Excess formaldehyde with base yields Bakelite — stoichiometry is a genuine lever
common_traps: Assuming phenol-formaldehyde polymerization always gives a 3D network — Novolac is linear and thermoplastic
Confusing the catalyst role: acid promotes electrophilic substitution at ortho/para; base promotes multiple hydroxymethylations and crosslinking
phenol_to_polymer_h.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (catalyst and stoichiometry control product type: thermoplastic vs thermoset)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: CH₂N₂ (diazomethane) gives O-methylation only; Me₂SO₄ (dimethyl sulfate) requires prior formation of phenoxide (PhO⁻Na⁺) for SN2 O-methylation

Substrate control: The regiochemistry of any subsequent EAS on the substituted benzene product is determined by the directing effects of the new OMe group (ortho/para director) and any pre-existing substituents
common_traps: Assuming phenol reacts with NaHCO₃ to give CO₂ — it does not; pKa phenol (~10) > H₂CO₃ (~6.35)
Assuming all nitrophenols react with NaHCO₃ — only those with pKa < ~6.3 (e.g., 2,4-dinitrophenol) do; mono-nitrophenols do not
Assuming the O-methylation step can cause C-methylation (Friedel-Crafts alkylation) — reagents CH₂N₂ and Me₂SO₄ are O-selective under these conditions
phenol_to_substituted_benzene_chndiazome.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (acid-base reactivity of phenol vs. carboxylic acids; reagent selectivity for O- vs. C-alkylation)

genuine_levers: Monomer must be electron-rich alkene (e.g., isobutylene, styrene, vinyl ethers) to stabilise carbocation — electron-poor alkenes (e.g., acrylonitrile) do NOT undergo cationic polymerisation
Lewis acid + trace H₂O (proton source) is essential for cationic initiation — anionic polymerisation requires strong base; radical polymerisation requires radical initiator (e.g., AIBN, peroxides)
common_traps: Assuming any alkene can undergo cationic polymerisation — only electron-rich monomers work
Confusing cationic (Lewis acid + H₂O) with radical (peroxide/heat) or anionic (organolithium) polymerisation conditions
Forgetting that termination step yields a neutral polymer chain, not a charged species
polymer_to_carbocation_lewisacidbfa.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (monomer selectivity: cationic vs anionic vs radical polymerisation)

genuine_levers: Monomer identity determines polymer properties: butadiene + acrylonitrile (BUNA-N) gives oil-resistant rubber; butadiene + styrene (BUNA-S) gives general-purpose rubber; chloroprene (Neoprene) gives oil/flame-resistant rubber
Polymerisation type: addition polymerisation (radical-initiated) vs. condensation polymerisation (e.g., nylon, polyester) — this is an addition polymer
common_traps: BUNA-N and BUNA-S are copolymers (two monomers), Neoprene is a homopolymer (one monomer) — students often misclassify them all as copolymers
Confusing 'BUNA' as a monomer — it is a brand name for butadiene-based polymers, not a chemical name
Incorrectly stating these are condensation polymers (they are addition polymers)
polymer_to_diene_conjugated_radicaliniti.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (monomer identification and polymer classification: homopolymer vs. copolymer, addition vs. condensation)

genuine_levers: Reaction is a step-growth polycondensation (eliminates NaCl), not a chain-growth addition polymerization
Requires primary alkyl halide (C-Cl) for efficient SN2 attack by disulphide nucleophile
Sodium disulphide (Na₂S₂) provides -S-S- linkages; changing to Na₂S would give monosulphide (-S-) linkages
common_traps: Mistaking it for vulcanization (sulphur cross-linking of natural rubber) — here, sulphur is in the polymer backbone
Confusing it with an addition polymerization — this is a condensation (eliminates NaCl)
Assuming the product is a thermoplastic like polyethylene — it's a specialized elastomer resistant to solvents
polymer_to_polymer_nassodiumdis.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (polymer classification: condensation vs addition; mechanism: SN2 polycondensation)

genuine_levers: Radical initiator (LDPE) vs. Ziegler-Natta (HDPE) determines polymer branching and properties (density, crystallinity)
Monomer structure (e.g., ethene vs. propene) determines polymer identity (polyethene vs. polypropene) and tacticity
Presence of peroxide/UV initiates radical mechanism, leading to branched LDPE; Ziegler-Natta gives stereoregular polymers
polymer_to_radical_radicaliniti.creates_stereocenters: conditional — Ziegler-Natta polymerization of unsymmetrical alkenes (e.g., propene) can create stereocenters along the chain, leading to isotactic/syndiotactic polymers
common_traps: Assuming all alkene polymerizations give the same product — LDPE and HDPE have different structures/properties due to mechanism
Forgetting that radical polymerization (LDPE) requires high pressure/temperature, while Ziegler-Natta (HDPE) works under mild conditions
Mistaking polymer repeat unit — it is the alkene monomer minus the π-bond, not the full alkene structure
polymer_to_radical_radicaliniti.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism and catalyst choice determines polymer structure and properties); Structural (monomer identity determines polymer identity)

genuine_levers: Initiator choice (benzoyl peroxide, acetyl peroxide, AIBN) determines radical generation rate and temperature needed (heat vs UV)
Termination mode (combination vs disproportionation) determines polymer end groups (saturated vs terminal alkene) and molecular weight distribution
High pressure conditions favor linear growth; low pressure favors chain transfer/branching (LDPE vs HDPE)
common_traps: Confusing initiator (consumed) with catalyst (regenerated)
Assuming all monomer converts to polymer (ignoring termination side reactions)
Forgetting that radical addition to unsymmetrical alkenes gives anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity (e.g., HBr/peroxides)
radical_to_radical_benzoylperox.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (radical chain mechanism: initiation/propagation/termination steps and their consequences)

genuine_levers: Reimer-Tiemann reaction requires a phenol (or highly activated benzene like aniline) — simple benzene or alkylbenzene does NOT react
Ortho selectivity is the major lever — if both ortho positions are blocked, reaction proceeds at para position
Dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂) is the electrophile, not CHCl₃ itself — generation requires NaOH and CHCl₃
common_traps: Applying this reaction to non-phenolic substrates (e.g., toluene) is wrong — it only works for phenols and similarly activated arenes
Forgetting that the product is an ortho-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde), not a simple benzaldehyde
Assuming the reaction works on any substituted benzene — it requires a strong activating group (-OH, -NH₂)
substituted_benzene_to_aldehyde_naoh.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: phenol vs other arenes; regioselectivity: ortho vs para)

genuine_levers: Ozonolysis of aromatic rings requires treating the Kekulé structure as a set of three C=C bonds; each bond is cleaved to give carbonyl fragments
The substitution pattern on the benzene ring determines the specific carbonyl products (e.g., o-xylene gives butane-2,3-dione and glyoxal)
common_traps: Forgetting that aromatic ozonolysis requires reductive workup (Zn) to prevent over-oxidation to carboxylic acids
Incorrectly applying alkene ozonolysis rules directly to benzene without considering the Kekulé structure and multiple cleavages
Mistaking the product as a single ketone instead of a mixture of carbonyl fragments from ring opening
substituted_benzene_to_alkane_o3.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (requires conceptual translation of aromatic ring into Kekulé structure for bond cleavage)

genuine_levers: Relative -M strength of substituents determines electrophilicity and reactivity order: NO₂ > COOR > CONH₂ > COO⁻
Anhydride formation requires a highly electrophilic carbonyl; substituents that strongly withdraw electrons (-M) promote reaction
common_traps: Mistaking COO⁻ (carboxylate) for a strong -M group; it is the weakest due to its pre-stabilized negative charge
Forgetting that two C=O groups in anhydride compete for electron density, affecting overall electrophilicity
Assuming amide (-CONH₂) is a stronger -M group than ester (-COOR); N lone pair donation makes it weaker
substituted_benzene_to_anhydride_seecond.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (ranking -M strength of substituents and predicting electrophilicity for anhydride formation)

genuine_levers: Kolbe-Schmitt reaction requires phenol (not benzene or alkylbenzene) — NaOH converts phenol to phenoxide, which then reacts with CO2
Counterion matters: Na+ phenoxide gives ortho product (salicylic acid); K+ phenoxide gives para product (4-hydroxybenzoic acid)
Ortho position must be free for the major ortho product; if both ortho positions are blocked, reaction gives para product
common_traps: Confusing Kolbe-Schmitt (phenol + CO2) with KMnO4 oxidation of alkylbenzene side chains (toluene → benzoic acid)
Assuming any substituted benzene can undergo this reaction — only phenols (or highly activated arenes like aniline) react
Forgetting that the carboxylic acid group is introduced at the ortho (or para) position relative to the existing OH group, not at the meta position
substituted_benzene_to_carboxylic_acid_1.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and substrate specificity: phenol vs other benzenes; ortho/para selectivity based on counterion)

genuine_levers: The reaction is the Cumene Hydroperoxide Process for phenol/acetone synthesis — the genuine lever is that the starting benzene must be isopropylbenzene (cumene). Any other substituted benzene will not give this specific ketone (acetone) as a product.
common_traps: Students may think this is a standard electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) reaction — it is not. The reagents O₂/H₂SO₄ do not install a ketone directly onto the ring via Friedel-Crafts acylation.
The 'notes' field mentions EAS regiochemistry, but this is a misdirection for this specific edge. The ketone (acetone) is a cleavage product, not a ring substituent.
substituted_benzene_to_ketone_oradicalin.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the Cumene Process as a specific industrial synthesis, distinct from typical aromatic substitution reactions)

genuine_levers: Step 1: sulphonation blocks ortho/para positions (C-2, C-4) to direct subsequent nitration to the remaining positions
Step 2: nitration with conc HNO3/H2SO4 replaces sulphonate groups (ipso substitution) and nitrates the remaining sites, ultimately yielding 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
Direct nitration of phenol with fuming HNO3 gives low yield due to oxidation of the OH group — the two-step sulphonation-nitration sequence is a yield lever
common_traps: Assuming phenol reacts with NaHCO₃ — picric acid does, but simple phenol does NOT (pKa threshold)
Thinking picric acid is a weak acid — it is a strong acid (pKa ~0.4) due to three -M NO₂ groups
Believing direct nitration of phenol is the best route to picric acid — it gives low yield; the two-step blocking method is superior
Forgetting that sulphonate groups are good leaving groups in ipso substitution by NO₂⁺
substituted_benzene_to_phenol_1conch2so4.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (multi-step synthesis with blocking groups and ipso substitution); Structural (regiochemistry control via sulphonation)

genuine_levers: Stepwise temperature increase is required for successive nitrations (50°C, 70°C, 100°C) — higher temperature overcomes ring deactivation by NO₂ groups
Methyl group is an ortho/para director, guiding the position of each successive nitration to yield the 2,4,6-trinitro pattern
common_traps: Assuming all three nitrations occur under identical conditions — the third nitration requires a higher temperature (100°C)
Forgetting that the methyl group is an EDG, which is essential for directing the nitration to ortho/para positions; a meta-director would give a different product pattern
Mistaking the product as a simple monosubstituted benzene — the product is a highly substituted, specific isomer (TNT)
substituted_benzene_to_substituted_benze.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (stepwise condition control and directing group effects in multi-step synthesis)

genuine_levers: Primary amines ONLY — secondary/tertiary amines give no reaction (distinguishing test)
CHCl₃ + alc. KOH generates dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂) — the reactive electrophile
common_traps: Aniline (ArNH₂) is a primary amine and DOES react — this is NOT an exception
Confusing this with Hinsberg test (which uses benzenesulfonyl chloride to distinguish 1°, 2°, 3° amines)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Dilute acid hydrolysis (HCl/H2O) yields primary amine + formic acid; strong base hydrolysis (KOH) yields primary amine + K-formate (salt) — product identity is the same, but the co-product is different
common_traps: Mistaking the co-product: The reaction yields formic acid (HCOOH), not CO2 or HCN
Confusing with nitrile hydrolysis: Nitrile (R-CN) hydrolysis yields carboxylic acid (R-COOH), not amine
isocyanide_to_amine_hydrolysis.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (distinguishing isocyanide hydrolysis from nitrile hydrolysis by co-product)

genuine_levers: O2/hv targets benzylic or allylic C-H bonds adjacent to the halide (activated positions) — standard alkyl halides without such activation do NOT give this product
Radical chain mechanism (autoxidation) — not a standard polar substitution or elimination pathway
common_traps: Mistaking this for a standard nucleophilic substitution or elimination reaction leading to an alkene or alcohol
Assuming the halide is directly converted to the carboxylic acid group (the halide is a spectator; oxidation occurs at an adjacent activated C-H)
alkyl_halide_to_acid_chloride_o2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing a non-standard, radical-based oxidation pathway on activated substrates)

genuine_levers: Lithium-halogen exchange is specific to aryl, vinyl, or activated alkyl halides; typical alkyl halides (e.g., 1-bromobutane) do not undergo this exchange efficiently
n-BuLi is a strong base but here acts as a nucleophile for exchange, distinct from its use in deprotonation
Product is a reactive organolithium (carbanion), not a Grignard reagent; reactivity and solvent (THF, -78°C) differ
common_traps: Confusing this with Grignard formation (Mg/ether) — Grignard works with alkyl halides, but lithium-halogen exchange prefers sp²/sp-hybridized halides
Assuming the product is a stable carbanion; it is highly reactive and must be used immediately in situ
Overlooking the requirement for low temperature (-78°C) to prevent side reactions like Wurtz coupling or decomposition
alkyl_halide_to_geminal_dihalide_rliorga.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (distinguishing lithium-halogen exchange from Grignard formation and deprotonation; substrate selectivity)

genuine_levers: Reaction is specific to methyl esters (CH3-CO-OR) via iodoform cleavage; other esters (e.g., ethyl) undergo simple saponification to carboxylate + alcohol
Excess NaOH and I2 are required for triiodination and cleavage; milder base (e.g., NaHCO3) would not drive the reaction
common_traps: Assuming all esters give iodoform test — only methyl esters do; ethyl acetate (EtOAc) is negative but its saponification product (EtOH) can give a false positive
Forgetting to acidify after reaction to obtain the free carboxylic acid (RCOOH) from the carboxylate (RCOO⁻)
Confusing this with haloform reaction of methyl ketones (CH3-CO-R') — here the leaving group is -OR, not -R'
ester_to_alkyl_halide_i2.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate specificity: methyl ester vs. other esters; iodoform mechanism vs. simple hydrolysis)

genuine_levers: Catalyst choice (H⁺ vs OH⁻) and reactant stoichiometry (excess phenol vs excess HCHO) determine polymer structure: linear thermoplastic Novolac vs cross-linked thermoset Bakelite
common_traps: Mistaking phenol as the product instead of a co-reactant consumed in the polymerization
Forgetting that the reaction is an electrophilic aromatic substitution on the phenol ring, not a simple carbonyl addition
Assuming all phenol-formaldehyde polymers are thermosets (ignoring the linear Novolac thermoplastic product from H⁺/excess phenol conditions)
aldehyde_to_phenol_hcatalystnov.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (catalyst and stoichiometry control polymer architecture)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: conc. H2SO4 + heat sulfonates tertiary C-H; SO2Cl2/hv (Reed reaction) gives alkanesulfonyl chloride from primary/secondary C-H
Substrate branching: reaction ONLY works for branched alkanes with tertiary C-H; straight-chain alkanes do not react
common_traps: Mistaking product for a substituted benzene (it is an alkanesulfonic acid)
Applying this reaction to straight-chain alkanes (it is not applicable)
Forgetting the heat (Δ) condition
alkane_to_carboxylic_acid_conch2so4.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: tertiary vs primary/secondary C-H; reagent selectivity: H2SO4 vs SO2Cl2/hv)

genuine_levers: Strong base choice (t-BuOK vs NaOH/KOH) influences carbene yield and reaction efficiency — t-BuOK preferred for steric hindrance and non-nucleophilicity
Substrate must be a haloform (CHX3) to undergo α-elimination — other carbanions do not form carbenes this way
common_traps: Assuming CHCl3 is the direct electrophile in Reimer-Tiemann — the actual electrophile is the generated dichlorocarbene
Predicting ortho substitution in Reimer-Tiemann when both ortho positions are blocked — para product forms instead
Overgeneralizing α-elimination to all carbanions — it is specific to trihalomethyl anions
carbene_to_carbanion_naohstrongba.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: α-elimination vs β-elimination; electrophile identity in Reimer-Tiemann); Structural (substrate requirement: haloform)

genuine_levers: Reduction stops at secondary amine (R-NH-CH3); further reduction to primary amine (R-NH2) is not possible with these reagents
Reagent choice (H2/Pt vs LAH) is a lever for mechanism (catalytic hydrogenation vs hydride transfer) but yields the same product
common_traps: Misidentifying product as primary amine (R-NH2) instead of secondary methylamine (R-NH-CH3)
Assuming the C≡N triple bond is reduced to a primary amine like a nitrile (R-CN) would be
isocyanide_to_amine_82.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing the unique reduction outcome of the isocyanide functional group)

genuine_levers: Lossen rearrangement: product is primary amine with one fewer carbon atom than the hydroxamic acid — skeleton changes
Isocyanate intermediate (common to Hofmann, Curtius, Lossen) — hydrolysis yields amine + CO2
