genuine_levers: Fehling reagent oxidises aliphatic aldehydes to carboxylic acids; aromatic aldehydes (e.g., benzaldehyde) are NOT oxidised — substrate scope is a genuine lever
Fehling reagent is specific for reducing sugars and α-hydroxy ketones; simple ketones (e.g., acetone) give no reaction — functional group selectivity is a lever
common_traps: Mistaking Fehling's for Tollens' test (Ag mirror vs Cu2O precipitate)
Writing CuO (black) instead of Cu2O (brick-red) as the precipitate
Forgetting that the product is the carboxylate salt (RCOO-Na+) in basic medium, not the free acid
aldehyde_to_carboxylic_acid_452.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate scope: aliphatic vs aromatic aldehyde discrimination)

genuine_levers: Requires an α-hydroxy ketone (secondary) to be oxidized; simple ketones (no α-OH) give no reaction
Secondary α-hydroxy ketone (with H on α-C) gives positive test; tertiary α-hydroxy ketone (no H on α-C) gives negative test
common_traps: Assuming all ketones give positive Fehling/Tollens tests (only aldehydes and α-hydroxy ketones do)
Forgetting that the α-hydroxy ketone must tautomerize to an enediol to act as a reducing agent
ketone_to_carboxylic_acid_455.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: distinguishing α-hydroxy ketones from simple ketones)

genuine_levers: Ring size (Blanc Rule): 1,4- and 1,5-diacids form 5-membered anhydrides readily; 1,6-diacids (adipic) require P2O5 or acetic anhydride; 1,2-diacids (phthalic) also form 5-membered rings easily
Catalyst requirement: Succinic acid forms anhydride on heat alone; longer chain diacids often require dehydrating agents (P2O5, Ac2O) or thionyl chloride
common_traps: Applying Blanc Rule incorrectly: Malonic acid (1,3-diacid) decarboxylates on heating, does NOT form a stable 4-membered cyclic anhydride
Forgetting intramolecular nature: This is NOT intermolecular esterification; it is intramolecular dehydration to form a cyclic anhydride
Confusing with lactone formation: Lactones are cyclic esters from hydroxy acids; this forms a cyclic anhydride from a diacid
carboxylic_acid_to_lactone_456.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (Blanc Rule application: predicting product based on diacid chain length and required conditions)

genuine_levers: Blanc Rule: dicarboxylic acid chain length determines ring size (n=4 → 5-membered ketone, n=5 → 6-membered ketone)
Reagent choice: Ca/Ba salt + Δ gives ketone; SOCl2 or Ac2O gives anhydride (different product)
common_traps: Assuming this reaction forms an anhydride (common for 5- or 6-carbon diacids like succinic/glutaric) — for adipic acid (n=4), ketone forms instead
Forgetting the loss of two CO2 molecules and writing the product as a dicarboxylic acid derivative
carboxylic_acid_to_ketone_457.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (Blanc Rule: predicting ring size from diacid chain length)

genuine_levers: Cis (Z) geometry of the two COOH groups is essential for intramolecular dehydration to a 5-membered lactone; trans (E) geometry prevents the reaction under mild heating
common_traps: Assuming both maleic (cis) and fumaric (trans) acids form the anhydride equally upon heating
Writing the product as a simple intermolecular anhydride (e.g., (RCO)2O) instead of the correct intramolecular 5-membered ring lactone (maleic anhydride)
carboxylic_acid_to_lactone_458.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (E/Z geometry dictates reaction feasibility and product structure)

genuine_levers: NaOH (aq) causes deformylation of alkynyl aldehydes (e.g., HC≡C-CHO) to alkynes; typical aldehydes (with α-H) undergo aldol or Cannizzaro
common_traps: Assuming all aldehydes with NaOH give aldol or Cannizzaro — alkynyl aldehydes are an exception giving alkynes
Confusing this with standard Cannizzaro (no α-H aldehydes give alcohol + acid salt) — here the product is an alkyne + formate
aldehyde_to_alkyne_461.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing alkynyl aldehyde as an exception to typical base-catalyzed aldehyde reactions)

genuine_levers: Reaction with dry CO2 yields carboxylic acid; reaction with other carbonyls (e.g., aldehyde, ketone, ester) yields alcohol — carbonyl identity is a genuine lever
common_traps: Confusing CO2 with other carbonyls (e.g., ester) which give alcohol products
Forgetting the acidic H3O+ workup step to protonate the carboxylate salt
Assuming the reaction adds two equivalents of Grignard (as with esters) — CO2 only adds one
grignard_to_carboxylic_acid_464.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (carbonyl electrophile discrimination: CO2 vs. other C=O groups)

genuine_levers: NaOH (aqueous) + heat drives irreversible saponification (BAC2) to carboxylate salt; acid workup (H3O+) is required to obtain free carboxylic acid
Ester hydrolysis under acidic conditions (H3O+, heat) is reversible and yields free carboxylic acid directly
common_traps: Writing the product as free carboxylic acid (RCOOH) directly without acid workup after basic hydrolysis — the base yields a carboxylate salt (RCOO-Na+)
Confusing saponification (base hydrolysis) with acid-catalyzed ester hydrolysis, which has a different (AAC2) mechanism and equilibrium
ester_to_carboxylic_acid_467.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism and product state: salt vs. free acid under basic vs. acidic conditions)

genuine_levers: Choice of halide (R-I > R-Br > R-Cl > R-F) affects reactivity; aryl/vinyl halides generally do NOT form Grignard reagents under standard conditions
Solvent choice: dry diethyl ether is standard; THF may be needed for less reactive chlorides
Strict anhydrous conditions are essential; any water destroys the Grignard reagent (RMgX + H2O → R-H)
common_traps: Assuming alkyl fluorides readily form Grignard reagents (they do not)
Forgetting that Grignard reagents are destroyed by protic solvents (H2O, ROH, NH3) and acidic hydrogens (terminal alkynes, carboxylic acids)
Writing a simple SN2 mechanism for formation (the actual mechanism is radical-based)
alkyl_halide_to_grignard_474.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent and condition sensitivity: halide reactivity, solvent, anhydrous)

genuine_levers: Choice of solvent: dry THF is required for aryl halides (higher bp, better solvation for ArMgX); Et₂O often insufficient for unreactive ArBr
Halogen identity: Aryl-Br and aryl-I form Grignards; aryl-F does NOT react with Mg under these conditions
Activation: Trace I₂ activates Mg surface; without it, initiation can be slow
common_traps: Assuming aryl-F can form Grignard (it does not)
Using wet solvent leads to immediate hydrolysis: ArMgBr + H₂O → ArH
Forgetting that vinyl halides (CH₂=CH-X) also form Grignards by the same method

genuine_levers: Grignard reagent is a strong base (pKa of RH ~45-50) → reacts with ANY protic source (H-A) with pKa < ~50 to give alkane R-H; the specific proton source (H2O, ROH, RCOOH, R-C≡CH, RNH2) does NOT change the product; the lever is the presence of any acidic proton, which destroys the Grignard
common_traps: Mistaking this acid-base reaction for a nucleophilic addition (e.g., thinking Grignard adds to the carbonyl of a carboxylic acid — it does not, it deprotonates the acid first)
Forgetting that Grignard is destroyed by trace water or alcohols in solvents — a common experimental trap
grignard_to_alkane_476.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard as a strong base, not just a nucleophile, and identifying all potential acidic proton sources)

genuine_levers: D2O vs. H2O — D2O gives deuterated alkane (R-D) for labelling; H2O gives protonated alkane (R-H)
common_traps: Mistaking this for a nucleophilic addition to a carbonyl — this is a simple acid-base protonation (deuteration) of the carbanion
Forgetting that the Grignard carbon is nucleophilic and reacts with ANY acidic proton source (H2O, ROH, NH3, terminal alkyne) to give alkane
grignard_to_alkane_477.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard as a strong base for acid-base reactions, not just a nucleophile for carbonyl addition)

genuine_levers: Terminal alkyne C-H (pKa ~25) is acidic enough to be deprotonated by Grignard reagent (RMgX) to form alkynyl Grignard; internal alkynes (no terminal H) do NOT undergo this reaction — substrate acidity is the lever
common_traps: Confusing this acid-base reaction with the typical nucleophilic addition of Grignard to carbonyls — here, Grignard acts as a base, not a nucleophile
Thinking the product is an addition product (e.g., R-MgX adding across the triple bond) instead of a substitution (H replaced by MgBr)
alkyne_to_grignard_478.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard reagent can act as a base with sufficiently acidic substrates, not just as a nucleophile)

genuine_levers: Primary alkyl halide (1°) required for SN2 coupling; secondary/tertiary halides give elimination
Grignard reagent acts as a strong base toward acidic protons (e.g., H₂O) to give alkane via protonation, not coupling
For efficient cross-coupling to form a new C–C bond, a Gilman reagent (R₂CuLi) is typically used instead of Grignard + alkyl halide
common_traps: Assuming Grignard + alkyl halide always gives clean SN2 coupling (yield is poor; Gilman route is better)
Confusing this with Wurtz reaction (which gives symmetrical alkane from two identical alkyl halides)
Mistaking the product as an alcohol (from carbonyl addition) instead of an alkane (from coupling or protonation)
grignard_to_alkane_480.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (distinguishing Grignard as nucleophile vs. base, and knowing Gilman reagent for cross-coupling)

genuine_levers: Grignard reagent acts as nucleophile in SN2-like displacement on molecular halogen (Br2) — the R group from Grignard becomes halogenated
Reaction is general for Cl2, Br2, I2 — choice of halogen determines product R-X
This is a method to introduce a halogen at the position of the Grignard carbon, useful for labelled compounds or when direct halogenation is not feasible
common_traps: Mistaking this for electrophilic aromatic bromination (Br2/FeBr3) — here the aromatic ring is already part of the Grignard reagent
Confusing with Grignard addition to carbonyls — this is a halogenation, not an addition
Assuming the Grignard carbon retains stereochemistry if chiral — the SN2-like step at the Mg-bonded carbon likely inverts configuration, but the product aryl halide is planar (sp2)
grignard_to_aryl_halide_481.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing Grignard as a nucleophilic carbanion equivalent for halogenation, not just for carbonyl addition)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Mg/dry ether with vicinal dihalide gives alkene via dehalogenation, NOT a Grignard reagent (which would require a non-vicinal alkyl halide).
Substrate specificity: Only vicinal dihalides (X-CH2-CH2-X) undergo this elimination; geminal dihalides or isolated halides give different products.
common_traps: Mistaking this for Grignard formation — vicinal dihalides with Mg eliminate to alkene, they do not form di-Grignard reagents.
Assuming the mechanism is a simple E2 — it proceeds via Mg insertion into one C-X bond followed by intramolecular β-elimination of MgX2.
vicinal_dihalide_to_alkene_482.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent role: Mg as reducing/eliminating agent vs. Grignard-forming agent)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: Mg/dry ether generates a carbene (:CH₂) from geminal dihalide; Zn(Cu) (Simmons-Smith) generates a carbenoid for cyclopropanation with alkenes
Substrate: Must be a geminal dihalide (both halogens on the same carbon) for alpha-elimination to carbene; vicinal dihalides give alkynes via double elimination
common_traps: Mistaking the product as a free, stable carbene; it is a reactive intermediate or carbenoid that immediately reacts (e.g., with alkenes to form cyclopropanes)
Confusing this with standard Grignard formation (RMgX from alkyl halide); here, the Grignard-like intermediate undergoes alpha-elimination
geminal_dihalide_to_carbene_483.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing geminal dihalide + Mg as a carbene source, not a standard Grignard reagent)

genuine_levers: Requires 2 equivalents of Li metal (unlike Grignard requiring 1 Mg) — one for electron transfer, one for product formation
Strictly inert atmosphere (Ar) and dry ether are essential — organolithiums are pyrophoric and react with moisture/air
Organolithiums are more reactive than Grignards (stronger C-Li bond, harder Lewis acid) — useful for making cuprates (EtLi + CuI → Et₂CuLi)
common_traps: Using only 1 equivalent of Li (like Grignard) is incorrect — mechanism requires 2 Li atoms
Confusing this with Grignard formation (R-X + Mg → RMgX) — reagents and stoichiometry differ
Ignoring the inert atmosphere — leads to decomposition (RLi + H₂O → RH + LiOH)
alkyl_halide_to_organolithium_485.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (stoichiometry and conditions: 2 Li vs 1 Mg, inert atmosphere, dry ether)

genuine_levers: Step 1: Alkyl halide + 2 Li → organolithium (RLi) — lithium-halogen exchange (not Grignard formation)
Step 2: 2 RLi + CuI → Gilman reagent (R₂CuLi) — stoichiometry (0.5 eq CuI) is critical; 1 eq CuI would give different copper species
Gilman reagent selectivity: 1,4-addition to α,β-unsaturated carbonyls (not 1,2-addition like RLi/RMgX); coupling with primary alkyl halides/acid chlorides (no reaction with tertiary alkyl halides)
alkyl_halide_to_organolithium_486.ez_propagates: conditional — if the alkyl halide is derived from an alkene, the E/Z geometry is lost during organolithium formation; however, if the organolithium/Gilman reagent is used in a subsequent stereospecific coupling, the product stereochemistry may be controlled by the substrate geometry in that step, not by this preparation step
alkyl_halide_to_organolithium_486.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the preparation step itself does not create stereocenters; however, if the starting alkyl halide is chiral, the organolithium/Gilman reagent may retain configuration (with possible inversion/retention depending on mechanism) or racemize
common_traps: Confusing Gilman reagent preparation with Grignard reagent preparation (Mg vs Li; ether solvent vs dry ether/inert atmosphere)
Assuming Gilman reagent (R₂CuLi) reacts like RLi/RMgX (e.g., adding 1,2 to ketones — it does NOT)
Forgetting the stoichiometry: 2 RLi + 0.5 CuI (or 1 RLi + 0.25 CuI per equivalent) to form R₂CuLi; incorrect ratios yield different copper complexes
alkyl_halide_to_organolithium_486.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent discrimination: RLi vs RMgX vs R₂CuLi; stoichiometry precision; application selectivity — 1,4 vs 1,2 addition)

genuine_levers: Gilman reagent (R2CuLi) couples with vinyl halides via oxidative addition/reductive elimination at Cu, giving stereospecific retention of alkene geometry — Grignard reagents (RMgX) do NOT react with vinyl halides under normal conditions
Reaction is stereospecific: (Z)-vinyl halide gives (Z)-alkene; (E)-vinyl halide gives (E)-alkene
common_traps: Mistaking Gilman reagent for Grignard reagent — Grignard reagents do NOT form from or react with vinyl halides under standard conditions
Assuming SN2 mechanism — vinyl halides are unreactive toward standard SN2; this is an organocuprate-specific oxidative addition/reductive elimination
Forgetting stereospecificity — the E/Z geometry of the vinyl halide is retained in the product alkene
vinyl_halide_to_alkene_488.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (organocuprate vs. Grignard selectivity; stereospecificity of vinyl coupling)

genuine_levers: MnO2 oxidizes ONLY allylic and benzylic alcohols (primary → aldehyde, secondary → ketone); simple aliphatic alcohols are inert
Reagent selectivity: MnO2 is milder than PCC and does NOT oxidize isolated alkenes or other sensitive groups
common_traps: Assuming MnO2 oxidizes all primary alcohols (it does NOT oxidize simple aliphatic alcohols like ethanol)
Confusing MnO2 with PCC (PCC oxidizes all primary alcohols to aldehydes, MnO2 is selective for allylic/benzylic)
alcohol_to_aldehyde_491.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: MnO2 vs PCC vs KMnO4 for benzylic/allylic oxidation)

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces ester to TWO primary alcohols (acyl part → R-CH2-OH, alkoxy part → R'-OH); NaBH4 does NOT reduce esters — reagent choice is a genuine lever
common_traps: Forgetting the second alcohol product (the alkoxy part R'-OH) is a common omission
Mistaking LiAlH4 reduction for NaBH4 reduction (NaBH4 reduces aldehydes/ketones but NOT esters)
Writing carboxylic acid as product (LiAlH4 reduces ester beyond aldehyde stage directly to alcohol)
ester_to_alcohol_494.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: LiAlH4 vs NaBH4; product count)

genuine_levers: LiAlH4 reduces amides to amines; NaBH4 does NOT reduce amides — reagent choice is a genuine lever
Amide type determines amine type: primary amide (RCONH2) → primary amine (RCH2NH2); secondary amide (RCONHR') → secondary amine (RCH2NHR'); tertiary amide (RCONR'R'') → tertiary amine (RCH2NR'R'')
amide_to_amine_495.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the resulting amine carbon (former carbonyl) becomes a stereocenter; requires that the R group and the new CH2NH2 group are different, and the other three substituents on nitrogen are distinct (rare for primary amines from primary amides)
common_traps: NaBH4 is incorrectly used for amide reduction — it only reduces aldehydes/ketones
Writing carboxylic acid as product — LiAlH4 reduces amide C=O to CH2, not OH
Mechanism involves tetrahedral intermediates and elimination of oxygen as oxide, not a simple nucleophilic addition like with aldehydes
amide_to_amine_495.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: LiAlH4 vs NaBH4 for carbonyl reduction); Structural (amide substitution pattern determines amine type)

genuine_levers: SeO2 oxidises benzylic CH2 to C=O (ketone); allylic CH2 to conjugated enone — substrate type (benzylic vs allylic) is a lever
SeO2 is selective for activated CH2 groups (benzylic, allylic); does NOT oxidise isolated aliphatic CH2 groups — substrate activation is a genuine lever
common_traps: SeO2 oxidation is NOT for aromatic ring cleavage (that's KMnO4/heat) — product is ketone, not carboxylic acid
SeO2 oxidises CH2 to C=O; if the benzylic carbon is CH3 (e.g., toluene), the product is aldehyde (not ketone) — oxidation stops at aldehyde for benzylic CH3 groups
substituted_benzene_to_ketone_501.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: SeO2 for activated benzylic/allylic positions vs other oxidants for aliphatic or over-oxidation)

genuine_levers: Regioselectivity: SeO2 oxidises the allylic -CH2- adjacent to the double bond; for substituted cycloalkenes, oxidation occurs at the α-position to the MORE SUBSTITUTED end of the double bond
Product is an α,β-unsaturated ketone (enone), NOT an aldehyde or carboxylic acid — this is a specific allylic oxidation
common_traps: SeO2 is NOT a typical oxidant for converting alcohols to carbonyls (like PCC) — it specifically targets allylic C-H bonds
The intermediate allylic alcohol (e.g., cyclohex-2-enol) is not the final product; it is further oxidised to the enone
Mechanism is an ene reaction and [2,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement, NOT a simple carbocation or radical pathway
cycloalkene_to_ketone_502.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (specificity of SeO2 for allylic oxidation and enone formation; regiochemistry in substituted cases)

genuine_levers: Barrier height: Nitrogen inversion barrier is ~6 kcal/mol (very low) → enantiomers interconvert at RT → racemisation. Contrast phosphines (~35 kcal/mol) → configurationally stable at RT → can be chiral.
amine_to_amine_510.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the starting amine is a chiral trialkylamine (N with three different alkyl groups and a lone pair). The step itself does not create a stereocenter; it inverts an existing one.
common_traps: Assuming chiral trialkylamines can be resolved at RT — they cannot due to rapid inversion.
Confusing nitrogen inversion with pyramidal inversion of other atoms (e.g., phosphorus).
Thinking a reagent is required — this is a unimolecular thermal process.
amine_to_amine_510.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (contrasting nitrogen vs. phosphorus inversion barriers and configurational stability)

genuine_levers: Ring strain (3-membered) dramatically increases inversion barrier vs. acyclic amines — this is the key lever that makes aziridine stereochemistry stable
Substituent size on nitrogen — bulky groups can further increase the inversion barrier, but the ring strain is the primary factor
common_traps: Assuming nitrogen inversion is always fast (like in acyclic amines) and that aziridines cannot have stable stereoisomers
Confusing this with epoxide ring-opening stereochemistry — this step is about configurational stability of the nitrogen itself, not a reaction of the ring
epoxide_to_epoxide_511.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (understanding how ring strain alters fundamental physical organic properties — barrier to inversion)

genuine_levers: Presence of adjacent conjugated π-system (e.g., vinyl, phenyl) for backbonding → determines degree of N inversion barrier lowering and planarity
Number of conjugated substituents on N (e.g., three vinyl groups in trivinylamine) → influences extent of sp² character and planarity
common_traps: Assuming all amines are sp³ pyramidal — conjugated amines (aniline, trivinylamine) have partial sp² character
Overlooking that backbonding (N lone pair → π*) flattens geometry without a formal reagent
Confusing this electronic effect with steric effects (e.g., SIP/SIR) that also influence basicity/geometry
amine_to_amine_512.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (electronic structure effects on geometry and basicity without a classical reagent)

genuine_levers: Acid-catalysed isomerisation (H⁺/heat) drives terminal alkene (pent-1-ene) to internal alkene (pent-2-ene) for thermodynamic stability
common_traps: Assuming pent-1-ene and pent-2-ene are interconvertible at room temperature (requires breaking π bond)
Confusing constitutional isomerism with stereoisomerism (E/Z)

genuine_levers: Systematic enumeration of constitutional isomers based on the propane backbone and distinct placement of three chlorine atoms
geminal_dihalide_to_trichloropropane_iso.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the generated constitutional isomer has a carbon atom with four different substituents (e.g., 1,2,3-trichloropropane is achiral; stereocenters are not inherently created by the enumeration process itself)
common_traps: Miscounting isomers by failing to recognize symmetry (e.g., counting 1,1,3 and 3,3,1 as different, or incorrectly proposing 2,2,2-trichloropropane)
Confusing constitutional isomerism with stereoisomerism (e.g., considering enantiomers of a chiral structure as separate constitutional isomers)

genuine_levers: The identity of the two substituents (X and Y) determines the physical properties (bp, mp, dipole) of the three positional isomers
The relative directing effects of X and Y (ortho/para vs meta directors) determine the major product in electrophilic substitution synthesis, but this edge is about isomerism, not synthesis
common_traps: Incorrectly stating there are 4 or more isomers for C6H4XY (e.g., counting 1,5- as distinct from 1,3-)
Confusing this with the synthesis question: 'How many isomers?' is distinct from 'How to make a specific isomer?'

genuine_levers: Requires catalyst or energy input to interconvert (e.g., acid-catalyzed protonation, radical conditions, photoisomerization) — thermal energy at RT is insufficient
Catalyst choice determines outcome: H⁺/Br⁻ leads to scrambling; H₂SO₄ favors thermodynamic trans product; NO₂/hν leads to cis→trans
common_traps: Assuming cis/trans isomers are freely interconvertible at room temperature — they are stable, distinct isomers without a catalyst
Forgetting the two conditions for geometric isomerism: (1) restricted rotation (C=C) AND (2) each C of the double bond must have two different substituents
alkene_to_alkene_517.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (requires understanding that geometric isomers are stable at RT and interconversion requires bond cleavage via specific conditions)

genuine_levers: D/L assignment (Fischer convention) is independent of R/S (CIP) assignment — they can differ
D/L is based on the configuration of a reference carbon (e.g., glyceraldehyde C-2) relative to D-glyceraldehyde, not on optical rotation (+/–)
common_traps: Assuming D always corresponds to (R) and L to (S) — this is only a coincidence for glyceraldehyde
Assuming D always means dextrorotatory (+) and L always means levorotatory (–) — optical rotation is measured, not deduced from D/L
Confusing Fischer projection (D/L) with CIP rules (R/S) — they are separate classification systems
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_525.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (distinguishing historical D/L notation from modern CIP R/S and from optical rotation +/–)

genuine_levers: Requires four different substituents across the C=C=C axis — if any two substituents on the same terminal carbon are identical, the allene is achiral
Axial chirality arises from perpendicular planes of the two double bonds — a single stereogenic center or a regular alkene cannot give this outcome
allene_substituted_to_allene_axially_chi.creates_stereocenters: conditional — true only if the allene has four different groups arranged such that no plane of symmetry exists; otherwise false
common_traps: Assuming all allenes are chiral — only those with four different substituents across the axis are
Applying point chirality (R/S) rules directly to axial chirality without using the axial priority system
Forgetting that two identical groups on the same terminal carbon create a plane of symmetry, making the molecule achiral
allene_substituted_to_allene_axially_chi.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing axial chirality conditions and distinguishing it from point/planar chirality)

genuine_levers: Atropisomerism — steric bulk of ortho substituents prevents free rotation about the biaryl bond, creating a chiral axis
The specific identity and size of the ortho substituents (e.g., -COOH, -NO2) are the genuine lever; smaller groups (e.g., -F) may allow rotation, erasing chirality
substituted_benzene_to_substituted_benze.ez_propagates: not applicable — no E/Z geometry in starting material
substituted_benzene_to_substituted_benze.creates_stereocenters: conditional — creates axial chirality (atropisomers) only if ortho substituents are sufficiently bulky to restrict rotation at room temperature
common_traps: Mistaking this for a reaction creating a sp3 stereocenter — the chirality is axial, not centered
Assuming any ortho-substituted biaryl is chiral — the substituents must be bulky enough to restrict rotation
Forgetting that enantiomers are isolable at RT only when rotation is sufficiently slow
substituted_benzene_to_substituted_benze.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognition of axial chirality/atropisomerism from steric hindrance)

genuine_levers: Requires an alpha-hydrogen on an sp3 carbon adjacent to the carbonyl — no alpha-H means no enolization
Acid-catalyzed (H+/H2O) vs base-catalyzed (OH-/H2O) mechanisms differ in protonation/deprotonation order, but both yield the same enol tautomer
common_traps: Enol is a minor tautomer for simple ketones; equilibrium lies heavily towards the keto form — do not draw it as the major product
Enolization is NOT a reduction or oxidation — the oxidation state of carbon does not change
For unsymmetrical ketones, two possible enols can form; the more substituted enol is generally more stable
ketone_to_enol_542.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (understanding the tautomeric equilibrium condition and the requirement for an alpha-hydrogen)

genuine_levers: Base-mediated enolization via a 5- or 7-atom cyclic transition state — requires a conjugated system with a gamma-H (or delta-H) relative to the carbonyl
Regiochemistry: the specific enol formed depends on which acidic H is abstracted (e.g., gamma vs delta) and the resonance stabilization of the resulting carbanion
common_traps: Assuming enolization occurs only at the alpha-carbon — in conjugated systems, gamma-H abstraction is possible and leads to a different enol
Forgetting that the enol is in equilibrium with the keto form; the product is a tautomer, not a stable final product
ketone_to_enol_543.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (identifying the correct acidic proton and cyclic transition state in conjugated systems)

genuine_levers: Reagent choice: OD-/D2O vs OH-/H2O determines deuterium vs hydrogen incorporation at alpha positions

Substrate structure: symmetrical ketone (e.g., acetone) leads to complete deuteration of all alpha positions; unsymmetrical ketone leads to stepwise deuteration at each alpha site; conjugated enone allows deuteration at gamma positions via 1,5-enolisation
common_traps: Assuming all hydrogens (including carbonyl or beta positions) are exchanged — only alpha (and gamma for enones) hydrogens are labile
Forgetting that the reaction is stepwise and reversible, requiring multiple cycles for complete deuteration
Mistaking the mechanism for a direct substitution instead of enolate formation followed by protonation/deuteration
ketone_to_ketone_544.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: enolisation kinetics and site selectivity based on substrate structure)

genuine_levers: Enol % depends on solvent polarity: high in non-polar (CCl4), low in polar protic (H2O) due to competing H-bonding
Enol % depends on substrate: 1,3-dicarbonyls (e.g., pentan-2,4-dione) have high enol % due to chelation and conjugation; simple ketones (e.g., acetone) have negligible enol %
common_traps: Assuming all ketones have significant enol content — only 1,3-dicarbonyls and other special cases do
Forgetting that enolisation is an equilibrium, not a reaction with a 'product' in the traditional sense
Mistaking the enol form as the major species in all solvents — it's only major in non-polar solvents for stabilised systems
ketone_to_enol_545.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (understanding factors affecting keto-enol equilibrium: solvent polarity, substrate stabilisation via chelation/conjugation)

genuine_levers: Aromaticity of the benzene ring provides extraordinary thermodynamic stabilization (~150 kJ/mol), making the enol (phenol) form overwhelmingly favored over the keto form — this is an extreme exception to the general keto>enol rule
common_traps: Assuming phenol is less stable than its keto tautomer due to the general keto>enol rule — here, aromaticity reverses the stability order
Applying Saytzeff's rule (more substituted alkene is more stable) to predict enol stability without recognizing that aromaticity is the dominant factor
ketone_to_phenol_546.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing aromaticity as a dominant thermodynamic driver that overrides general tautomer stability rules)

genuine_levers: Bridge length ordering (m >= n >= p) — a different set of bridge lengths defines a different bicyclic structure
Total carbon count formula (m+n+p+2) — changing m, n, or p changes the parent alkane name
common_traps: Misapplying the bridge length ordering rule (m >= n >= p) — incorrectly ordering the numbers leads to wrong IUPAC name
Forgetting to add the two bridgehead carbons in the total count (using m+n+p instead of m+n+p+2)
Incorrect numbering sequence: must start at a bridgehead and traverse the longest bridge first

genuine_levers: IUPAC numbering rule: start adjacent to spiro C in smaller ring, go through smaller ring first, then larger ring
Spiro carbon count: total carbons = m + n + 1, where m and n are ring sizes excluding the spiro carbon
Ring size order: m < n, where m is the smaller ring size (excluding spiro C)
common_traps: Forgetting the '+1' for the spiro carbon when calculating total carbons from m and n
Incorrect numbering: not starting adjacent to the spiro carbon in the smaller ring
Mistaking m and n: m is for the smaller ring, n for the larger ring (both exclude the spiro C)
spiro_compound_to_spiro_alkane_550.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (IUPAC nomenclature rules and systematic numbering)

genuine_levers: Choice of naming system: common names (e.g., tert-butyl) vs. systematic names (e.g., 1,1-dimethylethyl) — complex substituents require systematic names
common_traps: Using common names for complex substituents (e.g., 'sec-butyl' for a branched chain with more than 4 carbons) instead of systematic names
Incorrectly applying systematic names (e.g., misidentifying the parent chain in a substituent like 2-methylpropyl)
alkane_to_alkane_553.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (IUPAC nomenclature rules for substituent naming)

genuine_levers: IUPAC numbering direction choice — when two numbering directions give the same lowest locant set, alphabetical priority of substituents determines the correct name
common_traps: Numbering the ring to give the first substituent the lowest locant, ignoring alphabetical priority when locant sets are equal
Incorrectly applying alphabetical priority to the entire name rather than just to break the tie between identical lowest locant sets
cycloalkane_to_cycloalkane_555.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (application of IUPAC tie-breaking rules for cycloalkane substituent numbering)

genuine_levers: Primary vs. secondary vs. tertiary amine classification determines suffix vs. prefix naming
Longest chain containing the N-bearing carbon is the parent chain — a genuine lever if multiple chains exist
Lowest possible locant for the N-bearing carbon — a lever if the chain can be numbered from either end
common_traps: Using 'amino-' as a prefix for a primary amine (correct only when amine is a secondary functional group)
Incorrectly numbering the chain to give a lower locant to a substituent instead of the amine group
Failing to drop the final '-e' of the parent alkane before adding '-amine'
amine_to_amine_557.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (IUPAC naming rules: chain selection, numbering priority, suffix vs. prefix use)

genuine_levers: Parent chain selection: the longest chain attached to the nitrogen is the parent amine, not the longest chain in the molecule
Alphabetical ordering of different N-alkyl substituents in the name
Use of N,N- prefix for two identical substituents on nitrogen
common_traps: Incorrectly naming the parent chain as the longest carbon chain overall, ignoring the chain attached to N
Listing N-substituents in incorrect order (alphabetical, not by size)
Omitting the 'N-' locant for each substituent when they are different
Using 'di-' for two different substituents instead of 'N-...-N-...'
amine_to_amine_558.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (IUPAC nomenclature rules for substituted amines)

genuine_levers: Principal functional group hierarchy: aldehyde suffix (-al) vs. prefix (formyl- or oxo-) depends on presence of COOH or amide
Numbering priority: CHO carbon is C1 when aldehyde is principal; when COOH is principal, chain is numbered to give COOH the lowest locant, CHO gets a prefix locant
common_traps: Incorrectly numbering the chain when both COOH and CHO are present (e.g., making CHO C1 when COOH is principal)
Using the wrong prefix (e.g., 'formyl-' for a ketone or 'oxo-' for an aldehyde in the wrong context)
Forgetting that the CHO carbon is always included in the parent chain
aldehyde_to_aldehyde_559.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (IUPAC nomenclature rules: principal group selection and numbering priority)

genuine_levers: N-substitution pattern (primary vs. secondary vs. tertiary amide) is a structural lever that changes the name
Presence of other higher-priority functional groups (e.g., -CHO, -COOH) changes the suffix and numbering priority
common_traps: Incorrectly numbering the chain to give the carbonyl carbon a higher locant than other substituents
Omitting 'N-' prefixes for substituents on the amide nitrogen
Failing to alphabetize N-substituent prefixes correctly
Treating the amide nitrogen as part of the main chain for numbering
amide_to_amide_561.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (systematic naming rules, substituent priority, and complex numbering)

genuine_levers: Nomenclature depends on locants of the two carbons bridged by the oxygen atom (e.g., 1,2-epoxy vs 1,3-epoxy)
For polycyclic systems, the bridging oxygen's locants define the ring fusion (e.g., 3,5-epoxycyclohexanone is bicyclic)
common_traps: Incorrectly assigning locants (e.g., writing 2,3-epoxypropane instead of 1,2-epoxypropane)
Confusing common names (oxirane, oxetane) with IUPAC epoxy prefixes
Mishandling numbering in cyclic ketones (e.g., in 3,4-epoxycyclohexanone, the oxygen bridges C3 and C4 of the cyclohexanone ring)
epoxide_to_epoxide_564.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (IUPAC nomenclature rules for bridged systems)

genuine_levers: Nomenclature is the sole lever: changing the carbon chain length changes the specific dicarboxylic acid name (e.g., butanedioic vs pentanedioic acid)
common_traps: Confusing common names (e.g., succinic) with IUPAC names (butanedioic) or misapplying the mnemonic for chain length
Incorrectly applying the mnemonic to mono-carboxylic acids or other functional groups

genuine_levers: Nomenclature is a genuine lever: 'formylamino' vs 'acetylamino' (acetamido) vs 'benzoylamino' (benzamido) — the acyl group determines the prefix
Position of substitution on the parent chain — numbering changes the compound name
common_traps: Mistaking 'formylamino' (-NH-CHO) for an aldehyde group (-CHO) attached to carbon
Incorrectly using 'aminoformyl' (which would imply -CO-NH2, a carboxamide) instead of 'formylamino'
Forgetting that the nitrogen is part of the substituent, not the parent chain, leading to incorrect numbering
formylamino_group_to_formylamino_group_5.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (IUPAC nomenclature of substituted amides as prefixes)

genuine_levers: Position of substituents on the aromatic ring determines the IUPAC name (e.g., ortho, meta, para) and the common name (e.g., phthalic, isophthalic, terephthalic)
Presence of additional functional groups (e.g., -OH, -NH2) on the ring changes the common name (e.g., salicylic acid, anthranilic acid)
common_traps: Confusing common names with IUPAC names (e.g., writing 'benzoic acid' as the IUPAC name instead of 'benzenecarboxylic acid')
Mixing up the common names for ortho, meta, and para isomers (e.g., calling terephthalic acid 'isophthalic acid')
Incorrectly applying common names to non-aromatic carboxylic acids
carboxylic_acid_to_carboxylic_acid_573.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (nomenclature recall and positional isomer discrimination)

genuine_levers: IUPAC name vs. common name mapping — a specific structural feature (e.g., alpha-hydroxy, alpha-keto, phenyl substitution) defines the common name
common_traps: Incorrectly deriving the structure from the common name (e.g., confusing lactic acid (2-hydroxy) with pyruvic acid (2-oxo))
Assuming trivial names imply trivial structures (e.g., carbamic acid is H2N-COOH, not CH3-COOH)
carboxylic_acid_to_carboxylic_acid_574.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (nomenclature recall and structural feature identification)

genuine_levers: Reaction is a name-to-structure conversion; the lever is correctly identifying the IUPAC name from the common name (e.g., acrolein → prop-2-enal) or vice-versa
common_traps: Confusing similar common names (e.g., crotonaldehyde vs crotonic acid)
Incorrectly drawing the structure from the name, especially E/Z geometry for 'croton-' compounds
Mistaking functional groups (e.g., aldehyde vs acid, nitrile vs isocyanate)

genuine_levers: Br2/H2O oxidises ONLY the aldehyde group (CHO) to COOH; does NOT oxidise primary alcohol (CH2OH) — this is a key selectivity lever
Contrast with HNO3, which oxidises BOTH the aldehyde and the terminal primary alcohol to give a dicarboxylic acid
common_traps: Assuming Br2/H2O oxidises the primary alcohol CH2OH to COOH (it does not; that requires HNO3 or another strong oxidant)
Applying this reaction to a ketose (e.g., fructose) — Br2/H2O does NOT oxidise ketones, so no reaction occurs; this reaction is specific to aldoses
carbohydrate_to_carboxylic_acid_577.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant selectivity: Br2/H2O vs HNO3; also used as a diagnostic test for aldose vs ketose)

genuine_levers: Ring size (C5-OH attack gives 6-membered pyranose; C4-OH attack would give 5-membered furanose)
Anomeric configuration (alpha vs beta) — the new stereocenter at C1 is formed; alpha has OH axial (down in Haworth for D-sugar), beta has OH equatorial (up in Haworth for D-sugar)
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_578.creates_stereocenters: true — creates a new stereocenter at the anomeric carbon (C1); the alpha/beta configuration is determined during cyclization
common_traps: Incorrect Haworth projection: forgetting that 'right in Fischer' = 'down in Haworth' for D-sugars
Misassigning C6 (CH2OH) orientation — it is always UP in the standard Haworth projection for D-aldohexoses
Assuming the alpha anomer is more stable (beta is usually more stable due to equatorial anomeric OH)
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_578.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (Haworth/Fischer interconversion rules and anomeric stereochemistry)

genuine_levers: Ring opening via open-chain aldehyde intermediate — the anomeric carbon becomes achiral in the open form, allowing re-closure to either alpha or beta anomer
Equilibrium composition (alpha ~36%, beta ~64%) is a genuine lever — specific rotation changes from +112° (alpha) or +18.7° (beta) to +52.5° (equilibrium mixture)
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_579.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the anomeric carbon (C1) becomes a stereocenter upon ring closure, but its configuration (alpha or beta) is not predetermined; the step creates a mixture of anomers
common_traps: Assuming mutarotation is a simple epimerization — it is NOT; it requires ring opening to the open-chain aldehyde
Forgetting the open-chain intermediate exists at low concentration (~0.02%) — the mechanism hinges on this reactive species
Thinking the specific rotation is constant — it changes over time until equilibrium
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_579.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: ring opening/closing via open-chain intermediate; equilibrium composition)

genuine_levers: NaBH4 reduces the terminal aldehyde group to a primary alcohol, leaving internal secondary alcohols untouched — a selectivity lever for terminal CHO over internal OH groups
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_580.creates_stereocenters: conditional — the aldehyde carbon (C1) becomes a new stereocenter only if the starting carbohydrate is an aldose (like glucose); for ketoses, the ketone carbon is not terminal and may already be a stereocenter
common_traps: NaBH4 reduces aldehydes and ketones, but NOT carboxylic acids or esters — a common trap is to think it reduces all carbonyls
The product is an alditol (sugar alcohol) with CH2OH at both ends — a mistake is to write a cyclic hemiacetal form for the product
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_580.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: NaBH4 vs LiAlH4 vs catalytic hydrogenation; and structural recognition of aldose vs ketose)

genuine_levers: D/L configuration assignment depends on the orientation of the OH group at the penultimate (highest-numbered chiral) carbon in the Fischer projection
Changing the OH orientation at the penultimate carbon from right to left changes the carbohydrate from D to L configuration
common_traps: Confusing D/L with d/l optical rotation (D/L is configuration, not rotation)
Mistaking the anomeric carbon (C1) for the penultimate carbon when assigning D/L
Assuming D/L changes with ring formation (it is based on the open-chain Fischer projection)
carbohydrate_to_carbohydrate_582.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (Fischer projection analysis and D/L configuration assignment)

genuine_levers: Excess NH3 is essential to get primary amine product (amino acid); stoichiometric NH3 leads to secondary/tertiary amine formation via over-alkylation
SN2 at alpha-carbon bearing halogen — stereochemistry is inverted relative to starting alpha-haloacid (if chiral), but starting alpha-haloacid is racemic from HVZ step, so product is racemic
carboxylic_acid_to_amino_acid_587.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the alpha-carbon in the starting carboxylic acid is chiral before halogenation (rare); HVZ reaction creates a racemic alpha-haloacid, so the SN2 step yields a racemic amino acid
common_traps: Missing the HVZ step (alpha-halogenation) — students may try direct NH3 attack on carboxylic acid, which gives ammonium salt, not amino acid
Using stoichiometric NH3 leads to dialkylation (secondary amine) — a classic trap unless excess NH3 is specified
Forgetting the final acidification step (H3O+) to convert ammonium salt to free amino acid
carboxylic_acid_to_amino_acid_587.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent excess control: excess NH3 vs stoichiometric NH3); Structural (multi-step sequence: HVZ → SN2 → acidification)

genuine_levers: Sequential amide formation then intramolecular cyclodehydration — requires ortho-dicarboxylic acid substrate; meta- or para-isomers give linear polyamides, not cyclic imide
Heating is essential for cyclodehydration; without heat, only diamide (phthalamide) forms
common_traps: Assuming any dicarboxylic acid + NH3 gives a cyclic imide (only ortho-substitution allows 5-membered ring closure)
Confusing phthalimide (cyclic imide, acidic NH) with simple amide (neutral NH2) — phthalimide is a key Gabriel synthesis intermediate
carboxylic_acid_to_phthalimide_588.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate specificity: ortho-dicarboxylic acid required for cyclization; connection to Gabriel amine synthesis)

genuine_levers: SN2 at alpha-C of haloester — inversion of configuration if alpha-C is chiral
H3O+ hydrolyses both the imide N-C bond AND the ester group to give amino acid
Phthalimide anion acts as a protected ammonia equivalent — prevents polyalkylation (NH3 would give multiple substitutions)
phthalimide_anion_to_amino_acid_589.creates_stereocenters: conditional — if the alpha-C of the haloester is prochiral, SN2 creates a stereocenter; if it is already chiral, SN2 inverts configuration
common_traps: Forgetting that H3O+ hydrolyses BOTH the imide N-C bond AND the ester group — writing only amine or only carboxylic acid is wrong
Using NH3 instead of phthalimide anion leads to over-alkylation (primary, secondary, tertiary amines) — this is why Gabriel synthesis is used
Mistaking the mechanism as SN1 at the alpha-C (alpha-C is primary/secondary, but SN2 is favored due to good nucleophile and leaving group)
phthalimide_anion_to_amino_acid_589.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism: SN2 with stereochemical consequence; reagent role: phthalimide as ammonia surrogate); Structural (identifying final amino acid structure after dual hydrolysis)

genuine_levers: Homolytic cleavage of O-O bond under UV light (hν) generates acyloxy radicals; thermal or ionic conditions would not initiate this radical pathway
The acyloxy radical can undergo decarboxylation to form a phenyl radical, altering the downstream reaction pathway
common_traps: Assuming this is an ionic reaction like nucleophilic acyl substitution; it is a radical initiation step
Confusing the acyloxy radical (PhC(=O)O•) with a carbocation or carbanion intermediate
Overlooking the decarboxylation step (loss of CO2) that converts the acyloxy radical into a more reactive phenyl radical
anhydride_to_acyloxy_radical_597.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (recognizing radical initiation vs. ionic mechanisms; predicting subsequent radical chain reactions)

genuine_levers: The -M effect of the C≡N group directs electrophilic substitution to the meta position, overriding the ortho/para directing effect of the aromatic ring itself
The -I effect of the sp-hybridized carbon in C≡N contributes to overall deactivation of the ring towards electrophilic attack
common_traps: Incorrectly assigning the cyano group as an ortho/para director due to lone pairs on nitrogen — it is a meta director because the -M effect places positive charge on the ring
Drawing canonical structures where the positive charge is placed on the nitrogen atom of the nitrile group — the negative charge resides on nitrogen, making it a 'negative charge sink'
nitrile_to_cyano_arenium_ion_610.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (understanding the interplay of -M and -I effects to predict meta-directing deactivation)

genuine_levers: Priority of neutral, octet-complete structures over charged ones — this is the primary lever for ranking resonance contributors
Forbidden structures for F/O/N bearing a +ve charge with incomplete octet — this rule eliminates invalid canonical forms
common_traps: Drawing canonical forms where F/O/N has a +ve charge and only 6 electrons in its valence shell
Failing to prioritize a neutral, octet-complete structure over a charged one when both are valid
Applying secondary rules (like charge separation) before applying the primary octet/neutrality rule
carbocation_to_carbocation_612.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (ranking resonance structures by applying formal charge and octet rules in correct priority order)

genuine_levers: Regiochemistry: Markovnikov addition (H⁺ adds to less substituted C, OH ends up on more substituted C) vs. anti-Markovnikov addition (e.g., hydroboration-oxidation)
Reagent choice: H₂SO₄/H₂O gives Markovnikov alcohol; HBr/HCl (without peroxides) gives Markovnikov alkyl halide; HBr with peroxides (radical) gives anti-Markovnikov alkyl halide
alkene_to_alcohol_628.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carbocation intermediate is planar and the nucleophile (H₂O) attacks from either face, potentially creating a chiral center if the resulting sp³ carbon bears four different groups
common_traps: Assuming HBr always adds anti-Markovnikov (true only with peroxides/radical conditions; without peroxides, it's Markovnikov)
Forgetting carbocation rearrangements (hydride or alkyl shifts) possible with 2° carbocations, especially if a more stable 3° carbocation can form
Mistaking this for syn addition (it's not; stereochemistry is lost due to planar carbocation intermediate)
alkene_to_alcohol_628.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry: Markovnikov vs. anti-Markovnikov rules and reagent conditions); Structural (potential for carbocation rearrangement and chirality formation)

genuine_levers: Anti-Markovnikov regiochemistry — BH adds to less substituted carbon (steric control)
Syn addition — both H and OH add to the same face of the alkene
Oxidation step proceeds with retention of configuration at the carbon bearing boron
alkene_to_alcohol_629.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the carbon receiving the OH becomes a stereocenter (i.e., was originally a terminal alkene carbon that becomes chiral, or an internal alkene carbon that becomes chiral with four different substituents)
common_traps: Confusing with Markovnikov addition (e.g., H2O/H+) which gives the opposite regiochemistry
Forgetting the syn addition stereochemistry and drawing anti addition
Mistaking the oxidation step for an inversion process (it is retention)
alkene_to_alcohol_629.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (regiochemistry reversal and stereochemistry); Structural (if the product alcohol is chiral)

genuine_levers: Reagent selectivity: LAH reduces COOH to alcohol; NaBH4 fails — reagent choice is a genuine lever
BH3·THF selectively reduces COOH in presence of ester — chemoselectivity is a genuine lever
common_traps: NaBH4 (SBH) is incorrectly assumed to reduce carboxylic acids — it only reduces aldehydes/ketones
H2 with Pt/Pd/Ni is incorrectly assumed to reduce carboxylic acids — it fails; requires Ru catalyst
carboxylic_acid_to_alcohol_630.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (reagent selectivity: strong vs mild hydride agents; chemoselectivity of BH3·THF)

genuine_levers: Na metal reacts with all O-H/N-H bonds (alcohols, phenols, carboxylic acids, amines) — NaOH and NaHCO₃ react only with compounds more acidic than water and H₂CO₃, respectively
common_traps: Assuming NaOH reacts with alcohols (it does not — alcohols are less acidic than water)
Assuming NaHCO₃ reacts with phenols (it does not — only carboxylic acids and stronger acids like picric acid do)
alcohol_to_sodium_alkoxide_632.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (acid-base hierarchy: Na vs NaOH vs NaHCO₃ reactivity)

genuine_levers: Primary nitroalkane (with 2 α-H) → nitrolic acid; Secondary nitroalkane → pseudonitrole; Tertiary nitroalkane → no reaction — substrate classification is a genuine lever
Nitrolic acid has acidic N-OH proton → deprotonated by NaOH to red anion; Pseudonitrole lacks this proton → insoluble in NaOH (blue liquid) — product solubility/colour is a diagnostic lever
common_traps: Confusing nitrolic acid (R-C(-NO₂)=N-OH) with pseudonitrole (R₂C(-NO)(NO₂)) structure and properties
Forgetting that the red colour test (nitrolic acid) is specific to primary nitroalkanes reacting with HNO₂ and then NaOH
nitro_compound_to_nitrolic_acid_635.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (substrate selectivity: primary vs secondary vs tertiary nitroalkane; product identification via colour/solubility test)

genuine_levers: Choice of acylating agent (acyl chloride vs anhydride vs ketene) controls reactivity and byproducts
Number of -OH groups in unknown determined by MW increase (ΔMW = 42 per OH)
Phenol vs alcohol vs amine reactivity: selective acetylation possible with controlled equivalents
common_traps: Assuming phenol reacts with NaHCO₃ (it does not; only carboxylic acids and very acidic phenols like picric acid do)
Confusing MW calculation: net increase per -OH is 42 (C=24, O=16, CH₃ adds 15, loses H=1)
Thinking phenol gives a positive CAN test (it does not; only aliphatic 1°/2° alcohols give red)
alcohol_to_ester_637.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (acylating agent selectivity; MW calculation for OH counting; phenol vs alcohol vs amine reactivity)

genuine_levers: Requires bifunctional monomers (e.g., diol + dicarboxylic acid) for linear polymer; monofunctional acid yields simple ester, not polymer
Polyester (ester linkage) vs Polyamide (amide linkage) — choice of monomers (acid + alcohol vs acid + amine) determines polymer class
common_traps: Confusing condensation polymerisation (loss of small molecule like H2O) with addition polymerisation (no loss)
Assuming any diacid + any diol yields a fibre — fibre properties require specific monomers (e.g., terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol for PET) and crystalline domains
carboxylic_acid_to_polymer_644.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (monomer functionality and polymer classification: condensation vs addition, polyester vs polyamide)

genuine_levers: Migration aptitude order (Ar > R > H) is reversed vs SN1 (H > R > Ar) — a key lever
Within aryl groups, EDG-substituted (p-OMe) migrates faster than EWG-substituted (p-NO₂)
Within alkyl groups, migration aptitude follows carbocation stability (3° > 2° > 1°)
common_traps: Assuming SN1 migration order (H > R > Ar) applies here
Forgetting that the migrating group must be anti-periplanar to the leaving OH
Mistaking the product as an aldehyde when H migrates (it's still a ketone if the other carbon is tertiary)
diol_to_ketone_652.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (migration aptitude order reversal vs SN1; substituent effects on aryl migration)

genuine_levers: Electron-withdrawing group (EWG) on aryl ring accelerates reaction (inverse to typical 1,2-aryl shifts) — rate determined by initial OH⁻ attack on C=O
Electron-donating group (EDG) on aryl ring decelerates reaction — makes C=O less electrophilic, slowing the RDS
common_traps: Assuming EDG on aryl accelerates (as in Hofmann/Pinacol) — this is the key exception where EWG accelerates
Forgetting the 1,2-aryl migration step and writing direct nucleophilic substitution or oxidation
Confusing with base-catalyzed aldol or Cannizzaro reactions
ketone_to_carboxylic_acid_655.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (rate dependence on aryl substituent — EWG vs EDG effect is inverse to typical rearrangements)

genuine_levers: Ring size determines thermodynamic stability — 5- or 6-membered cyclic hemiacetal favored; 4-membered or larger rings disfavored
Equilibrium position is a genuine lever — open-chain aldehyde favored in aprotic solvents; cyclic hemiacetal favored in protic solvents or for sugars
aldehyde_to_hemiacetal_264.creates_stereocenters: conditional — a new stereocenter (anomeric carbon) is created ONLY if the attacking OH is NOT part of a symmetric diol; for monosaccharides, this creates α/β anomers
common_traps: Confusing hemiacetal (OH, OR) with acetal (OR, OR) — acetal requires full alcohol protection with acid catalyst
Assuming the equilibrium lies completely to one side — it is dynamic and solvent-dependent
Forgetting that the reaction is intramolecular — intermolecular hemiacetal formation requires a separate alcohol
aldehyde_to_hemiacetal_264.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (equilibrium control and anomeric effect in carbohydrate chemistry); Structural (ring-size prediction)
